title: Private deployment pricing discussion — Aurelia Health
preview:
From: Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@aureliahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Kyle Turner <kyle.turner@aureliahealth.com>, procurement@aureliahealth.com
Date: Thu, 3 Sep 2026 15:14:00 -0700
Subject: Private deployment fee breakdown and amortization question

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the demo earlier this week — the team was impressed with the runtime performance. Procurement and finance are asking for a clearer breakdown of costs before we proceed. Can you please provide:  

- Line-item for the VPC/on-prem license (per-node or per-seat?)
- Platform fee definition and whether it can be capitalized/amortized over 24–36 months
- Support tier options (SLA, oncall, response times) and per-tier pricing
- Any available discounts for 2- or 3-year commitments and migration credits

Attachment: Aurelia_RFP.pdf (contains the deployment requirements and expected peak QPS)

We need something we can share with legal and procurement by Monday (9/7). Appreciate a quick outline so we can align internally.

Best,
Emily Zhao
Director, Digital Platforms
Aurelia Health



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@aureliahealth.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 3 Sep 2026 17:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Private deployment fee breakdown and amortization question

Emily — thanks for the clear ask and for sending the RFP. Drafted a concise breakdown below and attached a pricing matrix and a starter SOW for your review.

Attachment: pricing_matrix_v2.xlsx, SOW_Aurelia_v1.pdf

Summary (high level):
- License: Per-node perpetual license (one-time) OR annual subscription (metered by committed GPU-hours). Typical customers with sustained usage pick the perpetual license + support.
- Platform fee: Monthly platform fee (covers control plane, telemetry, customer-specific routing rules) — normally billed as an OPEX monthly charge.
- Support tiers:
  - Standard: business-hours support, 24h ticket SLA, monitoring alerts — included with license.
  - Premier: 24/7 support, 4h critical response SLA, one quarterly on-site (or remote) review — +15% of license annualized cost.
  - Enterprise: Dedicated TAM, 1h P1 response, custom runbooks and on-call rotation — +25% of license annualized cost.

Discount guidance and credits:  
- 2-year commit: 10% off license or platform fee (customer choice)
- 3-year commit: 15% off license or platform fee + one-time migration credit (up to $75k)
- Pilot: we can provide a 90-day pilot with credits equivalent to up to 2 weeks of platform fee and waived onboarding retainer for production-qual pilots

Amortization note: The platform fee is typically OPEX but we recognize internal capex constraints. We can provide a structured invoice schedule that spreads an initial platform setup fee across 36 months for accounting purposes (we'll need procurement sign-off). See pricing_matrix_v2.xlsx for amortization examples.

Open items I flagged for legal/procurement:  
- Data residency & audit log retention (confirm retention windows)
- SLA credit calculation (how service credits apply)

Proposed next steps:
1) Review attached matrix and SOW with legal/procurement.
2) Tell us whether you prefer perpetual license vs annual subscription.
3) We prepare an amended SOW + DocuSign link for signatures.

Happy to hop on a 30-min call Friday if helpful.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123

On Thu, Sep 3, 2026 at 15:14 Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@aureliahealth.com> wrote:
> Hi Ben,
>
> Thanks again for the demo earlier this week — the team was impressed with the runtime performance. Procurement and finance are asking for a clearer breakdown of costs before we proceed. Can you please provide: ...



From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@aureliahealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 4 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Private deployment fee breakdown and amortization question

Emily / team — adding a few practical follow-ups from an implementation and commercial perspective: 

- For on-prem installs we price per-node (GPU) and define a baseline utilization band — overages are billed monthly at the agreed rate. If you expect bursty QPS, we recommend a small committed burst pool (licensed) + metered burst options.
- Platform fee floor: for small initial deployments we have a $5k/mo minimum platform fee; this is waivable with a 3-year commit (we can amortize the waived portion as migration credit).
- Oncall add-ons: 24/7 critical monitoring + pager rotation is typically $8k/mo as an add-on to Premier tier rather than built into Standard.
- SLA exceptions for health-care regulated deployments: we typically add an SOC2 scope extension and evidence package for an additional one-time fee (listed in the matrix).

Counter-proposal (commercial): If Aurelia signs a 3-year perpetual-license commitment with a migration plan in Year 1, we can increase the standard migration credit to $100k and provide the first six weeks of production support free. This is conditioned on agreeing to a standard amortization schedule in the SOW.

I'll be on the call Friday; if you want me to present the amortization examples I can walk through numbers.

—Marissa
Marissa Cole
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference

On Thu, Sep 3, 2026 at 17:45 Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Emily — thanks for the clear ask and for sending the RFP. Drafted a concise breakdown below and attached a pricing matrix and a starter SOW for your review.



From: Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@aureliahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Kyle Turner <kyle.turner@aureliahealth.com>, procurement@aureliahealth.com
Date: Fri, 4 Sep 2026 15:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Private deployment fee breakdown and amortization question

Ben / Marissa — this is helpful, thank you. A few follow-ups from procurement & finance: 

- Capitalization: Procurement is open to capitalizing the one-time license if we can show a 36-month amortization schedule on invoices (they prefer capex treatment). Can you provide explicit invoice lines that show the allocation across 36 months?
- Platform fee floor: $5k/mo is OK for initial pilots but we need the waiver language in writing tied to the 3-year commit.
- SLA: please confirm P1/P2 response windows and a sample credit calculation so the legal team can vet it.
- DocuSign: we prefer a single DocuSign for SOW + Annex A (pricing). If you can send that we can route to finance & legal.

Two small asks: can you push the migration credit to $100k (Marissa suggested this) and confirm whether the 90-day pilot credits stack with the migration credit or are mutually exclusive?

If we get the DocuSign + invoice schedule by Monday morning we can get procurement to issue a redline request.

Thanks,
Emily

On Fri, Sep 4, 2026 at 09:12 Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com> wrote:
> Emily / team — adding a few practical follow-ups from an implementation and commercial perspective: ...



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@aureliahealth.com>, Kyle Turner <kyle.turner@aureliahealth.com>, procurement@aureliahealth.com
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 5 Sep 2026 10:48:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Private deployment fee breakdown and amortization question — next steps

Emily / team — quick confirmation items and the next steps so procurement has what it needs: 

1) Migration credit: confirmed — we will honor a $100k migration credit if Aurelia executes a 3-year perpetual license commitment. Credit will be applied as a one-time invoice line and can be shown as an amortized contra-charge across 36 months for accounting transparency.

2) Pilot credits: the 90-day pilot credit (two weeks platform fee equivalent) is stackable with the migration credit but the migration credit applies to production invoice only (i.e., pilot credit is applied first; migration credit applies upon production go-live).

3) Platform fee amortization & invoice schedule: attached is a sample 36-month amortization schedule in pricing_matrix_v2.xlsx. We will produce an invoice that lists the one-time setup and the amortized platform allocation per month so your finance team can treat the setup as capital where allowed.

4) SLA and credits: P1 = 1 hour response (Enterprise), P2 = 4 hours (Premier), Standard = 24 business hours. Service credit calculation: credit = (SLO breach % * monthly platform fee) subject to exclusions in the SOW (see attached SOW_Aurelia_v1.pdf).

5) DocuSign: we prepared an SOW + Annex A bundle and will send a DocuSign envelope today: https://www.docusign.com/agree/ND-abcdef-123456

Action items for Aurelia:
- Confirm license model (perpetual vs annual subscription) by EOD Monday 9/7
- Provide legal redlines against the SOW in the DocuSign envelope once you receive it

If you want a quick reconciliation call on Monday morning (30 min) Jonas and I are available — otherwise we’ll wait for the signed SOW.

Thanks all,
Ben Carter
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123

title: GCP marketplace private offer — reseller crediting and burn schedule
preview:
From: CloudResell Ops <cpo@cloudresell.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Iris Wang <iris@acme.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 11:14:00 -04:00
Subject: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Hi Alex,

Thanks for the quick intro last week. Attached is a draft of the private offer terms for Acme AI (private_offer_terms.pdf). Acme confirmed the committed spend ($500,000) and asked us to propose a drawdown cadence that aligns to their procurement and forecast windows.

High level items we think matter for GCP co-sell / crediting integration: 
- Redemption window on the private offer (how long after activation they can redeem).
- Mapping of Redwood SKUs to GCP listing SKUs for usage attribution.
- Invoice phasing and how co-sell credits will be calculated (frontload vs amortize).

Can you share Redwood's preferred burn schedule options and who on your finance/partner team needs to be looped for the crediting mechanism? Our ops lead Jordan is available Wed/Thu for a quick sync.

Cheers,
Samir Patel
CloudResell Ops
CloudResell, Partnerships

--
Attachment: private_offer_terms.pdf (PDF, 248KB)


From: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 11:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Marissa / Ben — forwarding CloudResell's note and the draft terms from Samir. Acme is committed to $500k. They want us to propose a drawdown cadence that syncs to their procurement (PO timing) and CloudResell's redemption window. Questions I want us to resolve before we respond: 

1) Preferred redemption window (90 vs 180 days). Finance prefers 90 for revenue recognition simplicity but partners sometimes ask for longer.
2) Do we support frontloaded crediting (larger co-sell credit in months 1-3) or amortized across the committed period?
3) SKU mapping: we need a small mapping sheet for CloudResell so GCP can attribute usage. Marissa, can you take the SKU mapping? Ben, please advise on crediting and invoice phasing.

I'll propose a short call with Jordan + Samir tomorrow.

Thanks — Alex
Alex Martinez
Director, GTM Partnerships
Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 13:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Looping in a draft SKU mapping (see sku_mapping_sheet.xlsx). Notes / proposal: 
- SKU mapping: map GCP listing SKUs to Redwood product families (text-gen, chat, embeddings, dedupe). Keep aggregation at product family level to reduce mapping churn.
- Redemption window: recommend 90 days for the private offer start date; call this the "redemption window" in the private-offer terms. 90 days keeps invoicing clean for us and aligns with typical reseller workflows.
- Crediting: propose a hybrid approach — 60% of partner credit frontloaded across months 1-2, 40% amortized over months 3-6. This gives CloudResell an early incentive while smoothing our recognition impact.
- Invoice phasing: Redwood receives usage reports via GCP monthly; we will reconcile partner crediting monthly and document offsets on our invoices to CloudResell for the committed drawdown.

Open items for Ben: what tax / accounting flags do we need to call out for co-sell credits treated as contra-revenue? Also confirm if we need a Master Credit Agreement or if contract language in the private_offers doc suffices.

Thanks,
Marissa
Sr. Manager, Marketplace Partnerships


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 15:28:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Quick finance take: 
- Redemption window: 90 days is acceptable. We should document the exact trigger (offer activation date) and include a clause for exceptions with exec sign-off.
- Crediting treatment: co-sell credits will be recorded as contra-revenue. Frontloading 60/40 split is workable, but we need to ensure the partner-side payment terms (how CloudResell will be paid/credited) are explicit. If CloudResell expects a cash payout versus invoice offset, that changes our cash flow timing and requires treasury sign-off.
- SKU mapping: fine at product-family level. Please ensure the mapping includes the GCP SKU IDs (not just names) so attribution is granular enough for Google reports.
- Attachments: I'll need the final private_offer_terms.pdf and the PO from Acme to validate invoice triggers.

I'll be on the call at 10am PT tomorrow.

Ben Carter
Finance — Partnerships Billing


From: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
To: CloudResell Ops <cpo@cloudresell.com>, Iris Wang <iris@acme.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>, Procurement <procurement@acme.ai>
Date: Fri, 04 Sep 2026 16:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting — proposed schedule and next steps

Hi Samir / Iris — thanks for the draft. After internal review here is Redwood's proposed approach for Acme's $500k commit (deal RDW-2026-0047):

1) Redemption window: 90 days from private-offer activation.
2) Drawdown cadence (recommended):
   - Month 0 (activation): frontload 25% of expected committed units as a credit pool to CloudResell to enable early onboarding (up to $125k of usage value).
   - Months 1–2: additional 35% distributed across two months (~17.5% each).
   - Months 3–6: remaining 40% amortized evenly.
3) Crediting split: 60% of partner-eligible credit delivered in first two months (per Marissa's hybrid plan), 40% amortized months 3–6.
4) SKU mapping: Marissa attached sku_mapping_sheet.xlsx — please populate GCP SKU IDs where possible. We'll accept product-family mapping but GCP requires SKU IDs for attribution.
5) Invoicing / offsets: Redwood will reconcile monthly usage and apply partner credits as contra-revenue on invoices to CloudResell. If CloudResell requires cash payouts, we need a separate payout agreement and treasury approval.

Next steps:
- Samir: confirm that CloudResell can accept a 90-day redemption window and the frontload numbers above.
- Iris / Acme Procurement: please send the PO for the committed amount or confirm you will accept the private offer activation flow. If you can email PO to procurement@redwood.ai that will speed finance validation.
- Samir: if agreed, please route the final private offer through DocuSign (link recommended below) and attach signed MOP when complete.

We are happy to join a brief sync to finalize mapping and cadence. Proposed slot: Fri 9/4 10am PT (Jordan, Ben, Marissa, Alex available).

Best,
Alex Martinez
Director, GTM Partnerships
Redwood Inference

--
Attachment: sku_mapping_sheet.xlsx (XLSX, 56KB)
Attachment: acme_po_0926.pdf (PDF, 102KB)
Attachment: signed_mop.docx (DOCX, 34KB)

Looping in a draft SKU mapping (see sku_mapping_sheet.xlsx). Notes / proposal: 
- SKU mapping: map GCP listing SKUs to Redwood product families (text-gen, chat, embeddings, dedupe). Keep aggregation at product family level to reduce mapping churn.
- Redemption window: recommend 90 days for the private offer start date; call this the "redemption window" in the private-offer terms. 90 days keeps invoicing clean for us and aligns with typical reseller workflows.
- Crediting: propose a hybrid approach — 60% of partner credit frontloaded across months 1-2, 40% amortized over months 3-6. This gives CloudResell an early incentive while smoothing our recognition impact.
- Invoice phasing: Redwood receives usage reports via GCP monthly; we will reconcile partner crediting monthly and document offsets on our invoices to CloudResell for the committed drawdown.

Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>

Subject: Re: Fwd: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Quick finance take: 
- Redemption window: 90 days is acceptable. We should document the exact trigger (offer activation date) and include a clause for exceptions with exec sign-off.
- Crediting treatment: co-sell credits will be recorded as contra-revenue. Frontloading 60/40 split is workable, but we need to ensure the partner-side payment terms (how CloudResell will be paid/credited) are explicit. If CloudResell expects a cash payout versus invoice offset, that changes our cash flow timing and requires treasury sign-off.
- SKU mapping: fine at product-family level. Please ensure the mapping includes the GCP SKU IDs (not just names) so attribution is granular enough for Google reports.
- Attachments: I'll need the final private_offer_terms.pdf and the PO from Acme to validate invoice triggers.

Hi Samir / Iris — thanks for the draft. After internal review here is Redwood's proposed approach for Acme's $500k commit (deal RDW-2026-0047):

1) Redemption window: 90 days from private-offer activation.
2) Drawdown cadence (recommended):
   - Month 0 (activation): frontload 25% of expected committed units as a credit pool to CloudResell to enable early onboarding (up to $125k of usage value).
   - Months 1–2: additional 35% distributed across two months (~17.5% each).
   - Months 3–6: remaining 40% amortized evenly.
3) Crediting split: 60% of partner-eligible credit delivered in first two months (per Marissa's hybrid plan), 40% amortized months 3–6.
4) SKU mapping: Marissa attached sku_mapping_sheet.xlsx — please populate GCP SKU IDs where possible. We'll accept product-family mapping but GCP requires SKU IDs for attribution.
5) Invoicing / offsets: Redwood will reconcile monthly usage and apply partner credits as contra-revenue on invoices to CloudResell. If CloudResell requires cash payouts, we need a separate payout agreement and treasury approval.

Next steps:
- Samir: confirm that CloudResell can accept a 90-day redemption window and the frontload numbers above.
- Iris / Acme Procurement: please send the PO for the committed amount or confirm you will accept the private offer activation flow. If you can email PO to procurement@redwood.ai that will speed finance validation.
- Samir: if agreed, please route the final private offer through DocuSign (link recommended below) and attach signed MOP when complete.

We are happy to join a brief sync to finalize mapping and cadence. Proposed slot: Fri 9/4 10am PT (Jordan, Ben, Marissa, Alex available).

Best,

Director, GTM Partnerships

title: Phased commit & credit milestones for VPC deployment — pricing structure
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Jillian Park <jillian.park@acme-analytics.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 31 Aug 2026 09:22:00 -0700
Subject: Phased commit & credit milestones for VPC deployment — pricing structure

Hi Jillian —

Thanks again for the call this morning. Per our discussion I put together a concise phased-commit proposal so Acme can proceed with an internal approval path while preserving your cost predictability. Key points in the attached MAP-v3:

- Phase 1 (Pilot, 3 months): 50k tokens / mo baseline; Redwood provides $25k credit (one-time) applied against usage. No platform fee for pilot.
- Phase 2 (Ramp, month 4–9): 250k tokens / mo committed; 20% discount off list hosted rate; platform fee applies at a reduced rate (50% of list) starting month 4, waiver on overage for first two months of ramp.
- Phase 3 (Steady state, month 10+): full committed volume (custom), standard platform fee. Renewal pricing to be tied to performance metrics in MAP.

We believe this structure meets Acme’s need to limit upfront spend while giving Redwood visibility for capacity planning. Attachments: MAP-v3.xlsx (milestones + KPIs) and a high-level term sheet.

Next steps: if procurement is comfortable with the staged credits/fee schedule, can we get procurement to confirm the list of required legal clauses (data residency, audit access) so Priya can prepare a redline? Also looping Karthik for commercial approval on the proposed credit sizing.

Best,
Ava
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 415‑555‑0123

Attachment: MAP-v3.xlsx (milestones, metrics and credit schedule)

From: Jillian Park <jillian.park@acme-analytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Procurement Team <procurement@acme-analytics.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Sep 2026 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Phased commit & credit milestones for VPC deployment — pricing structure

Thanks Ava — this is helpful and straightforward. A few items procurement will need clarified before routing to our CFO (Tom):

1) Platform fee: we appreciate the pilot waiver. For months 4–9 we need the platform fee capped at $12k/mo (not percentage-based) unless we materially exceed the committed token ramp.
2) Credit timing: the $25k pilot credit — will that be invoiced and then refunded, or issued as an upfront credit on the first invoice? Please confirm accounting treatment.
3) Data residency & audit: we will require explicit language around audit windows and evidence (SOC2) for the on-prem package — please attach your SOC2 scope statement.

Lastly, we have a standard procurement checklist; if you can confirm we meet it, I can put this on Tom’s calendar for a decision next Wednesday.

—Jillian
Jillian Park
Head of Platform Engineering, Acme Analytics
jillian.park@acme-analytics.com

Cc: Jillian Park <jillian.park@acme-analytics.com>

Karthik / Priya — FYI, procurement pushed back on the platform fee and wants a hard cap for months 4–9. Also a question on credit timing (invoice vs. upfront). They indicated Tom wants to decide next Wed (9/8).

My read: we can accept a capped platform fee during ramp as long as the economics still cover the reduced rate + annualized committed volume (we get visibility). Suggested concession: cap at $12k/mo for months 4–9 in exchange for a firm 9‑month commitment (start of phase 2), and move the $25k pilot credit to an upfront credit applied to the first invoice (no refunds). That keeps billing simple.

Priya, can you advise whether the platform-fee cap and the credit-as-offset are acceptable from a legal/accounting perspective? Also we should confirm language around audit access and SOC2 scope — attaching the SOC2 scope statement they asked for.

If we can land legal on the clause set today, I’ll send a term-sheet update and ask Jillian to route to Tom.

Thanks,
Ava

From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 1 Sep 2026 13:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Phased commit & credit milestones for VPC deployment — pricing structure

Ava — quick thoughts from legal/finance:

- Platform fee cap: OK to agree to a fixed cap for months 4–9 provided we include an explicit usage reconciliation clause. If actual usage in the ramp window exceeds committed tokens by >25% for any month, Redwood reserves the right to reprice that month at a pre-agreed overage rate. Include a 30‑day notice window for any repricing.
- Credit treatment: prefer credit applied to first invoice (accounting simpler). Mark credit as non-refundable, non-transferable.
- Commitment trigger: the cap is tied to Acme signing a firm 9‑month committed volume for Phase 2 by 9/10/2026; if they fail to sign, cap void and standard platform fee applies retroactively (with an invoice for the difference).
- Audit/SOC2: confirm Redwood will provide the SOC2 scope statement (attached). For on‑prem/VPC we will include an agreed list of artifacts/controls and a redaction procedure; do not grant direct sysadmin access — provide evidence via secure share.

I’ll draft a short addendum to the term sheet capturing these points (credit treatment, cap + reconciliation, commitment trigger, audit evidence flow). If commercial agrees, I’ll circulate redlined PDF by EOD.

—Priya
Priya Natarajan
Sr. Counsel, Redwood Inference
priya.natarajan@redwood.ai

title: ACME - recommended link strategy for Redwood Private deployment
preview:
From: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-09-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Ben,

Thanks for the demo yesterday. Our security and network teams are evaluating how to connect our VPCs and on-prem datacenter to your Redwood Private control plane / inference nodes. Primary goals: private-only connectivity (no internet egress), predictable latency for inference (~<35ms ideally), and ability to support multiple AWS accounts and our on-prem CIDR space.

We have a relatively steady production load: roughly 5k-10k requests/sec during peak windows, average request payloads ~2KB, and occasional bulk batches that could spike sustained throughput to ~200 MB/s for short bursts.

Can you provide a recommendation: VPC Peering, AWS PrivateLink (interface endpoints), or site-to-site VPN? We also want any operational caveats, HA/failover guidance, and rough cost assumptions.

If you prefer a quick call, we can book a 30m slot with our NetOps.

Thanks,
Tom
Tom Sullivan
Director, Cloud Networking
ACME Corp


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
Date: 2026-09-01T10:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Tom —

Appreciate the details. Short answer: there isn't a single right option; each has tradeoffs. I can put together a short recommendation and cost/ops notes and send it back, or we can run a quick design call. If you can share whether you require transitive routing (i.e., do you want ACME VPCs to reach each other via Redwood's VPCs?) and whether Direct Connect is in scope, that will help.

I'll loop in our network engineers to get concrete configs and estimates.

-Ben
Ben Carter
Head of Solutions, Redwood Inference


From: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-09-01T10:14:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Ben — quick answers: no transitive routing desired (we don't want your infra to be a transit for our other VPCs), Direct Connect is not in scope for initial rollout (could be later). We'd prefer a written recommendation we can show to our security team. 30m call fine later if needed.

Thanks,
Tom


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
Date: 2026-09-01T11:02:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Kim/Jordan — forwarding ACME ask. They want a concise recommendation (Peering vs PrivateLink vs S2S VPN) with: 1) security boundary implications, 2) latency/bandwidth fit for their traffic profile (5k-10k req/s, occasional 200 MB/s bursts), 3) cross-account management (they have multiple AWS accounts), 4) failover plan for on-prem, and 5) an ops checklist for rollouts (endpoints per AZ, route table changes, monitoring).

Constraints: no transitive routing (per Tom), no Direct Connect initially, SOC2/compliance in play (so least-exposure to public internet is preferred). Attach two files: one-pager and their traffic estimates. Please reply-all with recommended approach and configuration details by EOD tomorrow — they'll likely include NetOps on the decision.

Forwarded message:
> From: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
> Date: 2026-09-01T09:12:00-07:00
> Subject: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request
> To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
>
> [original message quoted above]

Thanks,
Ben


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
Date: 2026-09-02T09:18:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Ben/Tom/Jordan —

High-level recommendation (summary):
- Primary: AWS PrivateLink (interface endpoints) into a dedicated Redwood endpoint service backed by NLB(s), one endpoint per ACME account/region (security boundary preserved).
- Secondary failover: Site-to-site VPN to Redwood-managed VPN concentrators (IPSec BGP) for on-prem tolerance and as a fallback path for transient PrivateLink capacity limits.
- When sustained high bandwidth is required later, evaluate Direct Connect (or Partner Interconnect) as a long-term augmentation.

Why this combination?
- Security: PrivateLink provides the tightest security model — ACME creates an interface endpoint in their account that connects to our endpoint service; we do not need to add routes to ACME route tables that would create transitive routing. PrivateLink endpoints are customer-controlled and do not expose our VPC as a transit hub.
- Cross-account: PrivateLink is designed for multi-account consumption without sharing route tables. Creating an interface endpoint per account keeps IAM/policy scoping straightforward.
- Latency & throughput: Peering is lower-latency and supports high throughput without the SNAT/HA considerations of PrivateLink, but peering requires more route table management and is not suitable if you want to avoid transitive routing or if accounts are in different regions. PrivateLink adds ~1-3 ms extra latency in practice if NLBs are in the same AZ, and throughput is limited by ENI/NLB scaling. Given your 200 MB/s spike requirement, PrivateLink can handle it if we: (a) deploy NLBs in each AZ with cross-zone enabled, (b) configure automatic ENI scale-out and warm-up testing, and (c) recommend connection aggregation (multiple interface endpoints) for bandwidth headroom. For sustained >1 Gbps, propose Direct Connect.
- VPN fallback: Managed IPSec tunnels are cheap and provide predictable failover for on-prem paths; they do add latency and variable throughput based on the endpoints and the carrier. Use BGP to advertise networks and BFD for faster failure detection.

Operational checklist (concise):
- Create a Redwood endpoint service (NLB-backed) per region; register NLBs in multiple AZs.
- ACME creates one interface endpoint per account + region; confirm security group inbound rules limited to ACME subnets.
- DNS: Use Private DNS entries (aws VPC endpoint private DNS) or provide a dedicated internal hostname via Route53 resolver rules so that clients target the endpoint IPs directly.
- MTU: set MTU to 1500 and validate path MTU; some VPN/encapsulation paths may require 1400.
- Monitoring: CloudWatch for NLB TargetGroup healthy hosts, ENI metrics, Latency percentiles; VPC Flow Logs for troubleshooting; implement synthetic SLO probes for p99 latency and throughput.
- IAM & certs: Use endpoint policies to restrict which principals can connect; mutual TLS at application layer for request-level auth.
- Rollout: Pilot in single account with 1 AZ, measure throughput/p99 latency, then expand to multi-AZ and additional accounts.

Cost considerations (ballpark):
- PrivateLink: charges for NLB hours + Interface Endpoint hours + data processing ($/GB). For heavy egress patterns this is ~2-5x the raw data transfer of peering, but buys stronger isolation and simpler per-account controls.
- VPC Peering: low per-GB transfer (intra-region) and less per-hour overhead, but operational surface area grows (route tables, security groups, cross-account sharing). Not recommended if you explicitly want to avoid transitive routes.
- VPN: low fixed-hourly cost for AWS side tunnels + data transfer; can be constrained at high throughput and adds latency. Good as secondary/failover.

Configuration snippets to include in the writeup:
- NLB listeners (TCP 443), target group type = IP, health checks / 10s, deregistration delay 60s.
- Endpoint policy allowing only ACME account IDs to accept connections.
- BGP on VPN with advertisements limited to ACME on-prem CIDRs; keep routes specific.

Fallback & resilience:
- If PrivateLink NLB saturates in an AZ, traffic will rebalance to other AZs; ensure cross-zone enabled and autoscale NLB target capacity.
- Use DNS-based client retry/backoff and client-side connection pooling to avoid per-call connection churn.

I'll draft a 1-pager (attached) and a short rollout checklist. If you want, Jordan can sketch the NLB sizing math based on their traffic spreadsheet.

-Kimberly
Kimberly Park
Network Architect, Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-09-03T16:40:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Tom —

Thanks for the patience. Per Kimberly: our recommendation is to lead with PrivateLink (interface endpoints) for initial rollout and use an IPSec site-to-site VPN as a failover path for on-prem connectivity. Key reasons: preserves your non-transitive constraint, gives account-level isolation, and aligns best with SOC2 expectations.

Next steps we suggest:
1) Pilot: create a PrivateLink endpoint in one ACME account + region, deploy Redwood NLBs across AZs, run load tests at your peak profile (5k-10k req/s, simulate 200 MB/s bursts).
2) Validate monitoring: p99 latency, NLB target metrics, ENI counts; iterate on NLB target scaling and client connection handling.
3) Configure IPSec VPN tunnels (BGP) to Redwood for on-prem fallback; test failover and MTU.
4) If sustained high bandwidth shows up, plan Direct Connect for the long term.

I've attached a one-page comparison and the spreadsheet with our initial capacity assumptions. Jordan will follow up with NLB sizing numbers if you want a more granular cost estimate.

Would you like us to open a shared Confluence / runbook and schedule a 30m tech call with NetOps this week?

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Head of Solutions, Redwood Inference
Attachments: privatelink-vs-peering-onepager.pdf, acme-mapping-traffic-estimates.xlsx

title: POC kickoff + live-traffic dry-run / capacity probe
Cross-region demo slotting & logistics - rotating weekly windows
preview:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Liam Ng <liam.ng@acme.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Cross-region demo slotting & logistics - rotating weekly windows

Hi Liam —

Thanks again for your time last week. We'd like to get the hands-on demo scheduled with your engineers, procurement, and security so we can walk through architecture, networking, and pricing. To make this workable across your EU/APAC/US stakeholders, we typically run a rotating weekly slot so no single region is always outside business hours.

Proposed approach:
- Two recurring 1hr sessions that alternate weekly: one timeslot optimized for APAC/EU overlap, the other for US/EU overlap.
- SE-led tech section (60–75 min) for VPC/network verification + live inference test.
- 15 min optional procurement/security mini-review on the first session in the series.

Please see the attached draft agenda (demo_agenda_v1.pdf). Can you share the right procurement and security contacts to loop in? I can set up a Doodle poll with 3 candidate windows; if you prefer a direct calendar invite we can do that as well.

Candidate windows (local):
- Option A (US-friendly): Thu 09:00–10:15 PT / Thu 17:00–18:15 BST / Fri 21:30–22:45 IST
- Option B (EU-friendly): Wed 16:00–17:15 BST / Wed 08:00–09:15 PT / Thu 20:30–21:45 IST

If those look reasonable I'll send the poll and loop in Avery for the tech checklist.

Thanks,
Jordan Blake
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
jordan.blake@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0182

Attachments: demo_agenda_v1.pdf


From: Liam Ng <liam.ng@acme.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Sarah Kwan <sarah_procurement@acme.com>, Tanvi Patel <tanvi.patel@globalsec.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Sep 2026 16:40:00 +0100
Subject: Re: Cross-region demo slotting & logistics - rotating weekly windows

Thanks Jordan — this looks sensible. Ill coordinate internally and copy procurement/security. A few quick notes from our side:

- Procurement (Sarah) will need a short window to review vendor terms and PO flow; shell confirm timing below.
- Security (Tanvi) wants to see the network checklist and confirm whether a dedicated VPC peering test can be included in the session.
- For our engineers, rotating is ideal. Our team prefers:
  * APAC-friendly weeks: 08:30–09:45 SGT (works for our Bangalore office on IST offsets)
  * EU-friendly weeks: 16:00–17:15 BST (works for London and Amsterdam)
  * US-friendly mornings are OK once every 3 weeks; wed rather the sequence be EU/APAC/US so no one location gets the worst slot every week.

Ive looped Sarah and Tanvi to confirm availability and any vendor onboarding steps. If you can share a Doodle, thatll speed things up.

Best,
Liam Ng
Director, Platform Engineering — Acme Corp
liam.ng@acme.com
+44 20 7946 0123


From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Liam Ng <liam.ng@acme.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Sep 2026 19:05:00 +0100
Subject: Re: Cross-region demo slotting & logistics - rotating weekly windows

Quick technical notes — thanks for the agenda, Jordan.

- We should plan for 75 minutes for the first session (60 min demo + 15 min connectivity troubleshooting). Subsequent sessions can be 60 min if the VPC peering and KV cache are verified.
- Please include a test dataset and a short list of example prompts (we can demo latency distribution with your real traffic pattern).
- Im attaching a condensed network checklist (network_checklist.xlsx) that covers firewall rules, allowed IP ranges, and required ports for VPC peering. If Tanvi can confirm the security proxy setup ahead of time, we can avoid a lot of friction in the live test.

title: Annual Prepay Degression & Capacity Fulfillment Plan — Mirage Analytics
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Priya Desai <priya.desai@mirageanalytics.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 19 Aug 2026 09:02:00 -0700
Subject: Annual Prepay Degression & Capacity Fulfillment Plan — Mirage Analytics

Hi Priya — nice to meet you (virtually). Per our call this morning, attaching a first-pass commercial outline for a multi-year committed arrangement that maps to Mirage's expected Dedicated footprint growth. High level: 3-year annual prepay with stepwise degression (unit price down as cumulative spend increases) and a phased GPU/TPM fulfillment schedule so you don't have to take peak capacity up-front.

Key bullets:
- Commitment: 3 years, annual prepay invoiced at the start of each year.
- Degression schedule (example): Year 1 baseline unit rate (100%), Year 2 -5% off baseline, Year 3 -8% off baseline; additional volume thresholds yield incremental discounts (see sheet).
- Ramp (phased fulfillment): Year 1 = 40% of contracted GPU-TPM capacity (to cover pilot + early prod), Year 2 = additional 35%, Year 3 = remaining 25% (we can reallocate within quarters upon 30-day notice).
- Overages: metered at the contracted unit rate + 10% premium unless you have pre-authorized burst credits.
- Early termination: amortized credits based on months of service consumed; propose 6-12 month amortization window.

Attachment: Commit_Schedule_v1.xlsx (spreadsheet) — contains a line-item forecast, sample amortization schedule, and two alternative discount ladders (conservative and aggressive).

Questions for you:
1) Do you prefer annual invoicing (single prepay per year) or a front-loaded multi-year invoice (annual commitments but billed upfront)?
2) What is Mirage's expected sustained token-per-minute (TPM) distribution by quarter? We used your April projections but want to confirm the Q1:Q2:Q3:Q4 split for capacity sizing.
3) Any procurement/finance constraints on early-termination amortization or required payment terms (net 30 vs net 45)?

If you want, we can turn this into a short MP (mutual plan) and route a signature-ready term sheet once you confirm cadence and the ramp curve. Looping in Lauren for commercial approval on the degression table.

Thanks —
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0162


From: Priya Desai <priya.desai@mirageanalytics.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Samuel Ortiz <sam.ortiz@mirageanalytics.com>
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 10:12:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Annual Prepay Degression & Capacity Fulfillment Plan — Mirage Analytics

Thanks Ben — appreciate the clear outline. A few clarifying items from procurement and finance before we push internal approval: 

1) Billing cadence: Mirage prefers annual commitments but prefers to pay via quarterly invoices tied to annual commitment (we'd like to avoid a single large upfront outflow for our board). Is that something Redwood can support while keeping the same unit economics?

2) Overage handling: we need smoother overage mediation. Can we agree on a small buffer (e.g., 10% of committed capacity) for free bursting per quarter, with overage true-up only if sustained > 30 days?

3) Early termination: procurement asked whether the amortization could be back-loaded (i.e., lower amortization in years 1-2, steeper in year 3) to protect our cash in early phases.

4) Security/compliance: we will need SOC 2 Type II report and a description of audit logging for requests served from Dedicated instances (customer-managed VPC). Sam will follow up on the exact evidence list.

Also attaching internal comments (summary below) — happy to jump on a 30-min call next week to align on the contract mechanics.

—Priya
Priya Desai
Head of Cloud Engineering
Mirage Analytics
priya.desai@mirageanalytics.com

On Wed, Aug 19, 2026 at 9:02 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> [original message and attachment]


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Priya Desai <priya.desai@mirageanalytics.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Samuel Ortiz <sam.ortiz@mirageanalytics.com>
Date: Fri, 21 Aug 2026 08:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Annual Prepay Degression & Capacity Fulfillment Plan — Mirage Analytics

Priya — thanks for the quick review and the practical asks. Answers inline and an updated commercial note below.

1) Billing cadence: We can accommodate quarterly invoicing against an annual committed amount (i.e., you pre-commit for the year; we invoice 4x quarterly instalments at 25% each). Doing so changes the cashflow but does not materially change the unit economics; minor admin fee adjustment applies (0.5% of invoice total) to cover billing reconciliation. If Mirage requires Net 45, we can shift, but that will impact the amortization calculation in the early termination clause.

2) Burst buffer: OK to include a rolling 10% free burst allowance per quarter; sustained >30 days will trigger true-up at contracted rate + 5% (we can soften the premium from 10% in the draft).

3) Early termination amortization: We can structure a back-loaded amortization per your ask: months 1-12 = 10% amortization of prepaid credits per year; months 13-24 = 20%; months 25-36 = 70%. That preserves your early cash position while protecting Redwood for later-year consumption. We can also add a phased buy-down option if Mirage hits certain growth milestones.

4) Security evidence: We will share the SOC 2 Type II report and a Dedicated deployment evidence packet (network topology, logging sample). I’ll loop our security team if procurement needs anything further.

Commercial update (proposed):
- 3-year annual prepay; quarterly invoices (25% each) for each year's commitment.
- Degression: year-on-year additive discounts up to -12% by Year 3 if you meet growth milestones (detailed in attachment).
- Ramp: Year 1 = 40%; Year 2 = +35%; Year 3 = +25% with the option to reallocate within 45 days notice (we reduced the notice window per your prod timelines).

Attachment: SOW_draft_v1.pdf (proposal SOW + deliverables + onboarding milestones).

Looping in Lauren for formal pricing sign-off and Vivek for capacity feasibility — any tweaks we should make before I send a redline-able term sheet?

Best,
Ben

> On Thu, Aug 20, 2026 at 10:12 AM Priya Desai <priya.desai@mirageanalytics.com> wrote:
> > [procurement questions]


From: Priya Desai <priya.desai@mirageanalytics.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Samuel Ortiz <sam.ortiz@mirageanalytics.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 23 Aug 2026 11:05:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Annual Prepay Degression & Capacity Fulfillment Plan — Mirage Analytics

Ben / Lauren — following up after our procurement review. We like the structure but have a few redlines we will ask legal to propose: 

A) Invoice schedule: request the invoice terms be Net 45; we can accept quarterly invoicing but need the additional 15 days.
B) True-up clause: want a 60-day sustained threshold rather than 30 for overage billing.
C) Early termination: propose a fixed cap on termination charges equal to 9 months of committed spend in year 1, 6 months in year 2, and 3 months in year 3 (this is an alternative to amortization math — simpler for our CFO).
D) Security: procurement listed required evidence (SOC 2 Type II, encryption at rest descriptions, KMS integration notes). Sam will send a formal evidence request and a procurement checklist.

We can accept the proposed degression ladder if Redwood will:
- Add monthly consumption reports within 5 business days of month end; and
- Commit to a 30-day remediation SLA for production-impacting incidents during the ramp window.

If Redwood agrees to the above, Mirage is prepared to sign a signature-ready term and target onboarding for mid-Oct.

Thanks —
Priya

> On Fri, Aug 21, 2026 at 8:45 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> > [commercial update]


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Priya Desai <priya.desai@mirageanalytics.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Samuel Ortiz <sam.ortiz@mirageanalytics.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Sep 2026 16:47:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Annual Prepay Degression & Capacity Fulfillment Plan — Mirage Analytics

Priya / Sam — thanks for the clear redlines and the timeline. Quick summary and proposed path to sign: 

1) Invoicing: We can do quarterly invoicing on an annual commitment with Net 45 terms (accepted). We will reflect the minor billing admin fee in the term sheet and waive it if Mirage elects electronic funds transfer.

2) True-up threshold: Accepting 60-day sustained threshold for overage billing, with a rolling measurement window.

3) Early termination: We will convert to a capped termination charge per your proposal (9/6/3 months) instead of the amortization schedule; will include a clause for accelerated amortization if misuse or fraud is detected.

4) Reporting & SLA: Redwood will provide monthly consumption reports within 5 business days and a 30-day remediation SLA for production-impacting issues during the first 12 months of the ramp. After month 12 we revert to standard SLA terms (per the SOW).

Next steps: I’ll update the term sheet to reflect these agreed commercial points and circulate a DocuSign-ready term sheet for signature. Lauren — can you confirm final list price and that the degression ladder below is approved for signature? (Summary: baseline unit rate X, year 2 -6%, year 3 -10% conditional on growth milestones).

Attachment: Final_Term_Summary.txt (plain text summary for legal).

If all good, I’ll push the term sheet to the HubSpot deal and send the DocuSign link (included above). Target start: onboarding week of Oct 12.

Thanks —
Ben Carter

Redwood Inference

Doing so changes the cashflow but does not materially change the unit economics; minor admin fee adjustment applies (0.5% of invoice total) to cover billing reconciliation. If Mirage requires Net 45, we can shift, but that will impact the amortization calculation in the early termination clause.

2) Burst buffer: OK to include a rolling 10% free burst allowance per quarter; sustained >30 days will trigger true-up at contracted rate + 5% (we can soften the premium from 10% in the draft).

3) Early termination amortization: We can structure a back-loaded amortization per your ask: months 1-12 = 10% amortization of prepaid credits per year; months 13-24 = 20%; months 25-36 = 70%. That preserves your early cash position while protecting Redwood for later-year consumption. We can also add a phased buy-down option if Mirage hits certain growth milestones.

4) Security evidence: We will share the SOC 2 Type II report and a Dedicated deployment evidence packet (network topology, logging sample). I’ll loop our security team if procurement needs anything further.

Commercial update (proposed):
- 3-year annual prepay; quarterly invoices (25% each) for each year's commitment.
- Degression: year-on-year additive discounts up to -12% by Year 3 if you meet growth milestones (detailed in attachment).
- Ramp: Year 1 = 40%; Year 2 = +35%; Year 3 = +25% with the option to reallocate within 45 days notice (we reduced the notice window per your prod timelines).

Hi Avery,

- Target pilot start: Oct 12 (2-week eval, then expand)

- Desired reserved capacity: 4 x A100-equivalent GPUs for the pilot week, scale to 12 if we move to production
- Procurement lead time: our purchasing team expects ~45 days for contract review + PO creation
- Security gating: SOC 2 in-scope, plus export controls review

Mosaic Health

From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.ai>
To: Diane Flores <diane.flores@mosaichealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-29 11:03 -0700
Subject: Re: Pilot activation timeline & capacity ask

Thanks Diane — forwarding an initial MAP and a couple of options for capacity. See attached \"Mosaic_MAP_v3.pdf\" and the procurement checklist. High-level summary below and next steps:

Summary (short):
- Recommended pilot reservation: 4 x A100-equivalent (dedicated) for a 2-week eval + staging window.
- Production ramp (if pilot accepted): staggered tranches of +4 GPUs every 30 days to reach 12 GPUs by month 3.
- Preferred path: Dedicated (reserved) capacity to guarantee latency SLOs; fallback path to Hosted with autoscale during procurement delays.
- Lead times: Dedicated booking typically needs 6–8 weeks to secure committed capacity in our Dedicated pool; Private (VPC/on-prem) add ~4–6 additional weeks depending on network onboarding and KMS setup.

Acceptance & gating (proposed):
1) Staging acceptance window: 48 hours smoke + 72 hours load test at 80% expected token throughput.
2) Quality gate: prompt/Eval suite pass rate >= 92% on agreed prompt set.
3) Security: SOC 2 evidence checklist provided; we recommend starting evidence exchange now so procurement can run in parallel.

Options if procurement slips:
- Short-term Hosted burst: move pilot onto Hosted API with soft QPS throttles and prefix caching to match latency (no reservation) — typically deployable in <3 days.
- Hybrid: Reserve smaller Dedicated tranche (2 GPUs) to meet immediate SLO, add Hosted capacity for tail traffic.

Next steps I propose:
- Ben will drop a GPU-sizing note with concrete throughput estimates and acceptance test plan.
- I’ll share a MAP doc with milestones, responsible owners, and procurement checkpoints.

Attachments: Mosaic_MAP_v3.pdf (draft MAP), procurement_checklist.xlsx

Regards,
Avery Johnson
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
(415) 555-0194

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Diane Flores <diane.flores@mosaichealth.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-30 09:42 -0700
Subject: Re: Pilot activation timeline & capacity ask

Hi Diane — quick sizing and test plan based on the numbers you provided.

Assumptions:
- 40 concurrent sessions peak
- 250k tokens/day (avg) with 2 peak hours (bursty pattern)
- Model: open-llama-13b-ggml (baseline) or equivalent (we can recommend a smaller/larger model depending on quality needs)

Recommended reserved configuration (pilot):
- 4 x A100-equivalent GPUs (dedicated): supports ~65 concurrent medium-latency sessions with continuous KV cache for each session.
- Batching config: dynamic batching with max batch size 32 and 100ms batching window to balance latency/cost.
- Estimated token throughput: 300k–400k tokens/day comfortably with the 4 GPU tranche.

Throughput/latency expectations:
- Median token latency: 10–15ms/token at steady state; tail latencies dependent on prompt length.
- Load test plan: simulate 80% and 120% of expected peak for 72 hours.

Lead times & procurement contingencies:
- Dedicated reservation provisioning (internal): 6 weeks lead time (typical) from signed order to committed tranche in our Dedicated pool.
- Private/VPC: add 4–6 weeks for network/KMS onboarding, certificate exchange, and audit baseline.
- Hosted fallback: 48–72 hours to configure production routing and burst capacity (no long-term reservation).

Acceptance test checklist (brief):
- Smoke: end-to-end API calls, model response sanity, KV cache behavior
- Performance: 72-hour load test at 80% expected peak; 95th percentile token latency under 2x median
- Quality: eval suite of 200 prompts; pass >= 92% (per earlier)

I’ll include a short Jenkins job and a k6 script in the onboarding artifacts. Happy to jump on a quick session with your infra team to walk the plan through.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

Attachment referenced: procurement_checklist.xlsx

- Recommended pilot reservation: 4 x A100-equivalent (dedicated) for a 2-week eval + staging window.
- Production ramp (if pilot accepted): staggered tranches of +4 GPUs every 30 days to reach 12 GPUs by month 3.
- Preferred path: Dedicated (reserved) capacity to guarantee latency SLOs; fallback path to Hosted with autoscale during procurement delays.
- Lead times: Dedicated booking typically needs 6–8 weeks to secure committed capacity in our Dedicated pool; Private (VPC/on-prem) add ~4–6 additional weeks depending on network onboarding and KMS setup.

Acceptance & gating (proposed):
1) Staging acceptance window: 48 hours smoke + 72 hours load test at 80% expected token throughput.
2) Quality gate: prompt/Eval suite pass rate >= 92% on agreed prompt set.
3) Security: SOC 2 evidence checklist provided; we recommend starting evidence exchange now so procurement can run in parallel.

Options if procurement slips:
- Short-term Hosted burst: move pilot onto Hosted API with soft QPS throttles and prefix caching to match latency (no reservation) — typically deployable in <3 days.
- Hybrid: Reserve smaller Dedicated tranche (2 GPUs) to meet immediate SLO, add Hosted capacity for tail traffic.

Next steps I propose:
- Ben will drop a GPU-sizing note with concrete throughput estimates and acceptance test plan.
- I’ll share a MAP doc with milestones, responsible owners, and procurement checkpoints.

Attachments: Mosaic_MAP_v3.pdf (draft MAP), procurement_checklist.xlsx

Regards,
Avery Johnson
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
(415) 555-0194

Hi Diane — quick sizing and test plan based on the numbers you provided.

Assumptions:
- 40 concurrent sessions peak
- 250k tokens/day (avg) with 2 peak hours (bursty pattern)
- Model: open-llama-13b-ggml (baseline) or equivalent (we can recommend a smaller/larger model depending on quality needs)

Recommended reserved configuration (pilot):
- 4 x A100-equivalent GPUs (dedicated): supports ~65 concurrent medium-latency sessions with continuous KV cache for each session.
- Batching config: dynamic batching with max batch size 32 and 100ms batching window to balance latency/cost.
- Estimated token throughput: 300k–400k tokens/day comfortably with the 4 GPU tranche.

Throughput/latency expectations:
- Median token latency: 10–15ms/token at steady state; tail latencies dependent on prompt length.
- Load test plan: simulate 80% and 120% of expected peak for 72 hours.

Lead times & procurement contingencies:
- Dedicated reservation provisioning (internal): 6 weeks lead time (typical) from signed order to committed tranche in our Dedicated pool.
- Private/VPC: add 4–6 weeks for network/KMS onboarding, certificate exchange, and audit baseline.
- Hosted fallback: 48–72 hours to configure production routing and burst capacity (no long-term reservation).

Acceptance test checklist (brief):
- Smoke: end-to-end API calls, model response sanity, KV cache behavior
- Performance: 72-hour load test at 80% expected peak; 95th percentile token latency under 2x median
- Quality: eval suite of 200 prompts; pass >= 92% (per earlier)

I’ll include a short Jenkins job and a k6 script in the onboarding artifacts. Happy to jump on a quick session with your infra team to walk the plan through.

Cheers,
Ben Carter

title: BrightBridge: VPC packaging, platform fee amortization & on-call retainer
preview:
From: Emily Tan <emily.tan@brightbridge.health>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement@brightbridge.health, Matthew Lowe <matthew@cloudscale.io>
Date: 2026-08-30T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: VPC pricing ask — platform fee amortization and on-call options

Hi Avery,

Thanks for the demo last week. The executive team wants a clear commercial path for a private VPC deployment — specifically we need: a) how the platform fee can be amortized across the term, b) options for a migration/implementation retainer versus a time-and-materials approach, and c) on-call/SRE retainer tiers (what’s included vs. priced add-on).

Two constraints from procurement: BrightBridge prefers amortization over at least 24 months and a clear cap on year-over-year increases. We’re also evaluating whether a partial waiver of the migration retainer is available if we commit to a longer term or a minimum GPU-hour commitment.

Can you send an itemized example (platform fee, implementation retainer, support tiers) and a sample SOW? Also, procurement needs SOC2 evidence and a short list of compliance deliverables up front. I’ve attached a short compliance checklist.

Thanks — Emily
Emily Tan
Head of Cloud Strategy, BrightBridge Health
attachments: brightbridge-compliance-checklist.xlsx (spreadsheet)


From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Tan <emily.tan@brightbridge.health>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>, Matthew Lowe <matthew@cloudscale.io>
Date: 2026-08-30T11:01:00-07:00
Subject: Re: VPC pricing ask — platform fee amortization and on-call options

Hi Emily — great to hear from you and thanks for the checklist. I’ve attached a short commercial pack (BBH-MAP-v1.xlsx) and a draft SOW (SOW-draft-BBH.pdf) to give the team something tangible to review. High level options below — happy to walk through live.

1) Platform fee amortization (examples):
 - Standard amortization: platform fee paid upfront; we can amortize internally for you at 12, 24 or 36 month schedules for commercial purposes. Example numbers in the attached MAP show amortization over 24 months reduces the near-term cash hit by ~40% vs. upfront.
 - Cap on increases: we can cap platform fee increases at 5% year-over-year for the committed term.

2) Implementation / migration retainer:
 - Option A: Fixed migration retainer (recommended for predictable scope): covers project mgmt, runbook handoff, 8-week implementation window, knowledge transfer.
 - Option B: Reduced retainer + T&M: smaller upfront retainer with time-and-materials for scope creep; we can discount the T&M if you commit to 18+ months.

3) Support/on-call tiers (sample):
 - Standard (included): business-hours (M-F 09:00–17:00) email + ticket triage; 24‑hour P1 response via ticket.
 - Premium (add-on): 24/7 pager-backed on-call with SRE; guaranteed P1 RTO 4 hours; includes 2 emergency incident responses/year without extra charge.
 - Enterprise (SLA): Dedicated SRE block + runbook updates; priced as monthly retainer.

Suggested commercial compromise we’ve seen work: amortize the platform fee over 24 months, offer 50% off the migration retainer if customer commits to a 24-month term and a minimum committed GPU-hour band (see MAP). We can also include SOC2 artifacts and a short compliance packet — I’ve attached the SOC2 evidence request template (SOC2-evidence-request-2026.docx).

Next steps: I can turn the MAP into a single-page commercial summary for procurement. Would you prefer a 24-month amortization example or a 36-month one for your execs?

Best,
Avery Johnson
Head of Sales — Enterprise, Redwood Inference
attachments: BBH-MAP-v1.xlsx (spreadsheet); SOW-draft-BBH.pdf (doc)


From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-31T08:45:00-07:00
Subject: internal: margin / discount guidance for BBH

Avery / Sean — quick internal note before we finalize the summary for BrightBridge:
 - If we do 50% migration retainer discount + 24-month amortization, margin compresses ~6 points but still within acceptable band given potential expansion (Horizon workload).
 - Recommend gating the migration retainer waiver/discount on either: (a) 24-month term with 60% of expected commit in year 1, or (b) 18-month term + committed GPU-hour floor.
 - Consider offering a tiered SRE block (e.g., 40 hrs/month at discounted rate) for the first 6 months to reduce initial op-ex friction.

I’ll update the MAP to include a blended price column (post-discount) and a quick note for legal re: audit log retention (they asked about retention windows — default is 90 days; for longer retention we’ll need to add storage fee).

Marissa

Thanks Avery — this is helpful. A few follow-ups from procurement and cloud ops:

 - BrightBridge preference is 24-month amortization — please send the exact monthly cash flows for that schedule so our FP&A team can model.
 - For the migration retainer: procurement would consider a 50% reduction if we sign 18–24 months and include a minimum committed baseline for the first 12 months. Can you confirm a firm number for the reduced retainer and the baseline GPU hours?
 - On on-call: we like the Premium option but need to know the exact scope of the 2 included emergency incidents (severity definition, time spent cap).
 - SOC2: procurement needs to see actual artifact examples; your SOC2 template is useful, but please share the last 12 months of third-party attestations or a portal link where we can pull redacted reports.

If you can get a clean commercial one-pager and the monthly amortization schedule by COB tomorrow we’ll route to legal and procurement for an initial read.

Emily


From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Tan <emily.tan@brightbridge.health>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>, Matthew Lowe <matthew@cloudscale.io>
Date: 2026-09-01T13:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: VPC pricing ask — platform fee amortization and on-call options

Emily — appreciate the specificity. Quick answers and a compromise proposal below; I’ll push a one-pager and the 24-month monthly cashflow table to your inbox by EOD.

 - 24-month amortization: will provide monthly schedule (platform fee / months). We can hold year-over-year increases to a maximum of 5% for the contracted term.
 - Reduced migration retainer: 50% discount available if BrightBridge signs a 24-month agreement and commits to a minimum of 120k GPU-hours in year 1 (numbers in the MAP attachment). Reduced retainer = $60k (vs. $120k full). If you prefer an 18-month term, we can do a $75k retainer with the same GPU-floor.
 - Premium on-call: covers 24/7 incident detection, pager escalation, and SRE-led incident remediation. The two included emergency incidents are defined as Sev 1 (service-impacting) and include up to 8 hours of SRE time each; additional hours billed at discounted T&M rate.
 - SOC2 artifacts: we can provide redacted attestations via a secure portal link; I’ve asked legal to prepare a redacted pack and will share the URL in the commercial one-pager.

Compromise suggested in the one-pager: 24-month amortization + $60k reduced migration retainer + 2 included Sev1 incident responses and a 10% committed-term discount on the recurring platform fee. If that aligns we’ll prepare an SOW and a term sheet for legal redlines.

Attaching updated SOW-draft-BBH.pdf (v2) for bracketed scopes and milestone payment schedule.

Thanks — Avery
attachments: SOW-draft-BBH.pdf (v2)

Subject: FedRAMP / ISO scope follow-ups for BoxCorp intake

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the intake call this morning. Two quick items we need to lock before the evidence sprint:

1) FedRAMP scope — you mentioned earlier that logging for Dedicated customers is retained in a customer-controlled bucket. Can you confirm whether Redwood will include audit logs for Dedicated vs Hosted in the same evidence set, or should we treat them separately for control sampling?

2) ISO control owner list — could you provide a single spreadsheet with control owners, evidence paths, and expected retention windows (especially for SOC 2 / SIG / CAIQ crosswalks)? If you have a draft, a shared Drive link is fine.

3) Timing — BoxCorp is targeting a production cutoff for new integrations 10/15; we need to know when the control evidence will be available for sampling so we can schedule auditors.

If it's easier, a short mapping (owner, location, link) is perfect.

Best,
Kelly

Hi Kelly / Sam —

Thanks for the clear asks. Summary below and attached is a first-pass mapping (controls-mapping-ava-v2.xlsx) and a draft FedRAMP scope PDF. Highlights:

1) Logging evidence sets
- Dedicated (customer-managed buckets): we'll provide a consolidated manifest that shows the bucket name, KMS key id, and a pointer to the stored audit logs. For sampling, please treat Dedicated customers separately — we will label evidence as \"Dedicated\" vs \"Hosted\" in the mapping so you can select the sample accordingly.

2) Control owner spreadsheet
- Attached spreadsheet includes control owners, primary contact, evidence path (Drive links or S3 manifest), expected retention (months), and whether there is a 3P attestation. Columns include SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / SIG / CAIQ crosswalks. Rachel created comments on rows where we anticipate engineering extraction.

3) Timing for BoxCorp sampling
- Earliest complete evidence handoff: 2026-09-10 for scoped controls that are already instrumented.
- Controls requiring engineering snapshots: target handoff 2026-09-24.

title: Deal surge: capacity playbook + OEM intake
preview:
From: Alex Dunn <ops@scalevision.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-29T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Sizing ask and timeline visibility for Dedicated onramp

Hey Ben —

Thanks again for the demo last week. We have a Q3 feature push that will increase inference traffic ~3.5x during peak hours and the product team wants a firm onboarding window. Procurement asked us to get a concrete hardware lead-time plan and OEM quotes so they can start approvals. Can you share a recommended dedicated-pool sizing (GPU counts + expected baseline throughput) and an intake plan that lines up with vendor lead times?

Attached is a short requirements doc (ScaleVision_MA-Requirements.pdf) with expected concurrency and retention. Happy to hop on a quick call.

Thanks,
Alex
Head of Ops — ScaleVision

---


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Dunn <ops@scalevision.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-29T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Sizing ask and timeline visibility for Dedicated onramp

Alex —

Appreciate the doc. High level we can support the 3.5x surge but there are two levers that matter for delivery timing: (1) OEM/GPU procurement windows for reserved on-prem or dedicated pool sizing, and (2) cloud backstop capacity we can reserve in advance as a temporary buffer.

Quick read: given your concurrency numbers, I’d propose a staged onramp:
- Phase A (T+4 weeks): reserve a modest dedicated pool to cover 60% of expected baseline — fast to provision if we align to cloud backstop + small reserved OEM tranche.
- Phase B (T+8–12 weeks): scale to full committed pool (covering 100% peak) once OEM quote is finalized and customs/lead-times are confirmed.

I’ll loop Karthik and Laura to map OEM quotes & procurement SLAs, and Vanessa to confirm cloud backstop pricing & reservation options. Can your procurement contact share preferred OEM list?

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
---


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-30T08:45:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Sizing ask and timeline visibility for Dedicated onramp

Team —

Reviewed Alex's requirements. Two immediate asks from procurement side we should surface to ScaleVision:
1) OEM lead times vary 8–14 weeks from PO depending on SKU and regional queue; expedited slots exist but carry premium and partial deposit.
2) Shipping + customs -> add 1–3 weeks depending on ship lane.

Action items I propose:
- Karthik: request firm OEM quotes (preferably 3 SKUs) with explicit ETA windows and deposit terms.
- Vanessa: model a 6-week cloud backstop reservation that can take overflow for Phase A. Include estimated delta in unit cost.
- Ben: get procurement contact and preferred OEM list from ScaleVision.

I'll draft a short procurement note that we can hand to ScaleVision procurement to accelerate sign-off.

Laura
Director, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference
---


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Jill Park <jill.park@oemgpu.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-31T10:22:00-07:00
Subject: OEM quote request: ScaleVision (DEAL-20894)

Hi Jill —

Looping you on a request from ScaleVision (prospect). They’re evaluating a dedicated GPU onramp with an initial staging pool and a full committed tranche later. Can you confirm availability and ETA for the following SKUs and provide a firm quote (including deposit terms and expedited slot pricing):
- SKU-A (A100-equivalent) x 24
- SKU-B (H100-equivalent) x 12
- SKU-C (L8-equivalent) x 36

Target timelines: Phase A (T+4 weeks) partial delivery for SKU-C and SKU-B to enable testing; Phase B (T+8–12 weeks) full delivery. If you have a fast-lane option, please specify lead time and cost.

We’ve attached a one-page sizing playbook (RV-dedicated-sizing-playbook.md) and the ScaleVision requirements doc to help.

Thanks,
Karthik
Head of Procurement Operations
Redwood Inference

---


From: Jill Park <jill.park@oemgpu.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-09-01T14:10:00-07:00
Subject: Re: OEM quote request: ScaleVision (DEAL-20894)

Karthik / team —

Attached is our preliminary quote (OEM_quote_v3.xlsx). Short summary:
- SKU-C (L8-equivalent): available in 3–5 weeks for volumes below 50 units.
- SKU-B (H100-equivalent): lead time 9–12 weeks, expedited lane available at +18% and 2-week deposit to secure slot.
- SKU-A (A100-equivalent): limited availability, ETA 12–16 weeks unless we allocate from a near-term batch (subject to 25% deposit).

Notes: expedited lanes dependent on current fulfillment cadence; final ETA confirmed on PO. Shipping estimates included; customs windows vary. Happy to jump on a call to walk through the workbook.

Best,
Jill Park
OEM Partnership Lead
---


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Dunn <ops@scalevision.com>, procurement@scalevision.com
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>, Jill Park <jill.park@oemgpu.com>
Date: 2026-09-02T15:04:00-07:00
Subject: Next steps: intake packet + provisional runbook (DEAL-20894)

Alex / ScaleVision procurement —

Thanks to Jill and the OEM team for the quick turnaround. Attached are the two items we discussed: a provisional intake/runbook and the OEM quote. Key recommendations to keep the Q3 launch on track:

1) Approve Phase A reservation (cloud backstop + small reserved SKU-C tranche) this week — enables baseline coverage in ~4 weeks.
2) Authorize a 10% deposit on an expedited SKU-B lane if ScaleVision wants the full performance profile in 8 weeks.
3) Accept our intake packet so we can kick procurement coordination (we will include customs/shipping buffer and a comms cadence).

If acceptable, I’ll send a short purchase guidance memo and calendar invites for a procurement sync (30m) and an Ops handoff (60m).

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
