title: Customer Security Questionnaire — Data Tenure & Erasure Commitments (Hosted API)
preview:
From: Alex Thompson <security@novacare.io>
To: Sofia Ivanova <sofia@redwood.com>
Cc: Dana Liu <dana.liu@novacare.io>
Date: 2026-08-30T15:22:00Z
Subject: Q: Data tenure & deletion — hosted API (security questionnaire)

Hi Sofia,

Thanks for taking this — attached is the short security questionnaire our compliance team asked us to return to internal counsel. We have two focused questions where we need explicit wording for our RFP: 

1) For requests sent to Redwood's hosted API, what is the default retention period for request and response content (including logs, traces, and cached KV)?
2) If a customer submits a written deletion request (for a tenant or specific identifiers), what is the end-to-end timeline and what artifacts do you provide to verify deletion?

We also want to understand backup/snapshot retention and whether deletions are propagated to backups. Please include the operational steps your team will follow and any enterprise-only options (e.g., contractually zero-retention or customer-managed key options).

Best,
Alex
Alex Thompson
Security Manager, Novacare Health

Attachment: novacare-security-questionnaire.pdf (PDF, 245 KB)

From: Sofia Ivanova <sofia@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-30T17:10:00Z
Subject: Fwd: Q: Data tenure & deletion — hosted API (security questionnaire)

Team — forwarding Novacare's two core asks. High level: we have to be clear and unambiguous. Ben, can you draft the technical timeline (logs, cache, snapshots)? Marissa, please add contractual wording options and what we can offer for an enterprise amendment (zero-retention / CMK).

I think we should include:
- Default retention policy for hosted API request/response material
- Backup & snapshot treatment and RTO/RPO constraints
- Exact deletion request workflow (support ticket, verification, SLA) and a sample deletion certificate we can hand to customers
- Any caveats (e.g., some platform telemetry retained for security investigations, legal holds)

Thanks — need a consolidated reply by Monday EOD.

Sofia Ivanova
Enterprise Security Lead, Redwood Inference

--- Forwarded message attached: novacare-security-questionnaire.pdf ---

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sofia Ivanova <sofia@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-31T09:06:00Z
Subject: Re: Fwd: Q: Data tenure & deletion — hosted API (security questionnaire)

Sofia / Marissa — draft technical answers below. I kept the wording consumable for legal/compliance but precise enough for our infra team: 

Default retention (hosted API):
- Requests and responses: by default, transient request/response payloads are retained for 30 calendar days to support abuse detection, model quality investigations, and operational debugging.
- Short-term traces/metrics (request id, timing, route): retained for 90 days for observability and incident correlation; these are separated from full payloads.
- Prefix/KV caches used for inference are ephemeral; cache entries persist only for the configured TTL (typically seconds to minutes). We do not persist KV cache snapshots for tenant request replay.

Deletion request workflow & timeline (tenant-level or identifier-level):
- Customer submits a verified deletion request via the Redwood Console or a support ticket referencing the tenant id and scope (e.g., \"all hosted API request/response payloads for tenant T-123\" or \"all records matching external_id X\").
- Verification step: Support performs requester verification (via account owner email + HubSpot/SSO check) within 24 business hours.
- Deletion execution: Within 5 business days after verification, we delete request/response payloads from the active datastore. Active datastore deletion is irreversible.
- Cache and short-term traces: cache entries expire within configured TTLs; short-term traces are scrubbed during deletion execution and removed from active indexing within 5 business days.
- Backups & snapshots: backups are taken for disaster recovery. Standard encrypted backups are retained up to 7 days in the default hosted plan and are on a rolling schedule. Deletions are applied to new backups after deletion execution; historical backups are automatically aged out and overwritten per the 7-day window. For customers requiring stricter guarantees, we offer an enterprise addendum to shorten backup retention windows or to exclude tenant data from backups where feasible.
- Delivery of evidence: After deletion execution we provide a signed Data Deletion Certificate (PDF) within 7 business days. Certificate contains scope, deletion timestamp, and the operator id who performed the deletion.

Enterprise options (contractual):
- Zero-retention hosted addendum: we can offer a contract that reduces payload retention to 0 days (no persistent storage of request/response payloads) but retains minimal telemetry (request ids and routing metadata) for 7 days for operational safety. This requires a minimum Dedicated or Private deployment commitment due to operational constraints.
- Customer-Managed Keys (CMK): available for encryption-at-rest for hosted enterprise customers; with CMK we will encrypt payloads with a customer key — deletion requires cryptographic key destruction per agreed process.

Caveats / legal holds:
- We cannot delete data subject to a lawful hold imposed by a court or governmental order. If a hold is presented, deletion will be paused and we will notify the customer with the hold reference.

Attachments I suggest we include in the customer reply: a short one-page deletion runbook (hosted-erasure-playbook.pdf) and a template Data Deletion Certificate. I uploaded both to the drive link in the metadata.

Ben
Ben Carter
Principal Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment stubs: hosted-erasure-playbook.pdf (PDF, 1.1 MB), data-deletion-certificate-template.docx (DOCX, 35 KB)

From: Sofia Ivanova <sofia@redwood.com>
To: Alex Thompson <security@novacare.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Dana Liu <dana.liu@novacare.io>
Date: 2026-09-01T14:42:00Z
Subject: RE: Customer Security Questionnaire — Data Tenure & Erasure Commitments (Hosted API)

Alex / Dana — thanks for the questionnaire. Below is our consolidated response you can include in your RFP. I've attached a short runbook and a sample signed deletion certificate for review.

Consolidated answers (hosted API):
1) Default retention windows: request/response payloads are retained for 30 calendar days by default. Observability traces and aggregated metrics are retained for 90 days. KV/prefix caches used for inference are ephemeral and follow configured TTLs (seconds–minutes).

2) Deletion process & timeline:
- Initiation: Customer submits deletion request via Console or support ticket indicating tenant id and scope.
- Verification: Redwood verifies requestor identity within 24 business hours.
- Active deletion: Data in active stores is deleted within 5 business days post-verification.
- Backups: Standard hosted backups have a 7-day rolling retention. Deleted data will no longer be present in new backups after active deletion; historical backups are aged/overwritten on the regular 7-day cadence.
- Evidence: A signed Data Deletion Certificate (PDF) is provided within 7 business days after deletion is completed.

Enterprise options (summarized):
- Zero-retention addendum (available on Dedicated/Private or enterprise-hosted addendum): reduces payload persistence to 0 days; requires operational acceptance testing and a minimum committed term.
- Customer-Managed Keys (CMK): available for encryption-at-rest; deletion workflows can incorporate key revocation steps where appropriate.

Caveats: legal holds and regulatory orders may supersede deletion timelines. Some minimal telemetry (routing ids, non-payload metrics) may be retained for short periods to support incident response unless explicitly contracted out.

Please see the attached runbook for the step-by-step operator actions and the template deletion certificate. If you want tightened backup retention or CMK right away, let us know and we can route to Commercial for an amendment and to Engineering for feasibility and scheduling.

Regards,

Sofia Ivanova
Enterprise Security Lead, Redwood Inference
sofia@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0184

Attachments: hosted-erasure-playbook.pdf, data-deletion-certificate-template.docx

On a related note — we will create a dedicated deletion ticket in our system (JIRA-OPS-4421) and attach the signed certificate there once completed.

- For CMK deletion: can you add a short note on how long after key revocation we consider data unrecoverable (best-effort timeline)?

Team — forwarding Novacare's two core asks. High level: we have to be clear and unambiguous. Ben, can you draft the technical timeline (logs, cache, snapshots)? Marissa, please add contractual wording options and what we can offer for an enterprise amendment (zero-retention / CMK).

I think we should include:

- Backup & snapshot treatment and RTO/RPO constraints

Sofia Ivanova
Enterprise Security Lead, Redwood Inference

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sofia Ivanova <sofia@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-31T09:06:00Z
Subject: Re: Fwd: Q: Data tenure & deletion — hosted API (security questionnaire)

Sofia / Marissa — draft technical answers below. I kept the wording consumable for legal/compliance but precise enough for our infra team: 

Default retention (hosted API):
- Requests and responses: by default, transient request/response payloads are retained for 30 calendar days to support abuse detection, model quality investigations, and operational debugging.
- Short-term traces/metrics (request id, timing, route): retained for 90 days for observability and incident correlation; these are separated from full payloads.
- Prefix/KV caches used for inference are ephemeral; cache entries persist only for the configured TTL (typically seconds to minutes). We do not persist KV cache snapshots for tenant request replay.

Deletion request workflow & timeline (tenant-level or identifier-level):
- Customer submits a verified deletion request via the Redwood Console or a support ticket referencing the tenant id and scope (e.g., \"all hosted API request/response payloads for tenant T-123\" or \"all records matching external_id X\").
- Verification step: Support performs requester verification (via account owner email + HubSpot/SSO check) within 24 business hours.
- Deletion execution: Within 5 business days after verification, we delete request/response payloads from the active datastore. Active datastore deletion is irreversible.
- Cache and short-term traces: cache entries expire within configured TTLs; short-term traces are scrubbed during deletion execution and removed from active indexing within 5 business days.
- Backups & snapshots: backups are taken for disaster recovery. Standard encrypted backups are retained up to 7 days in the default hosted plan and are on a rolling schedule. Deletions are applied to new backups after deletion execution; historical backups are automatically aged out and overwritten per the 7-day window. For customers requiring stricter guarantees, we offer an enterprise addendum to shorten backup retention windows or to exclude tenant data from backups where feasible.
- Delivery of evidence: After deletion execution we provide a signed Data Deletion Certificate (PDF) within 7 business days. Certificate contains scope, deletion timestamp, and the operator id who performed the deletion.

Enterprise options (contractual):
- Zero-retention hosted addendum: we can offer a contract that reduces payload retention to 0 days (no persistent storage of request/response payloads) but retains minimal telemetry (request ids and routing metadata) for 7 days for operational safety. This requires a minimum Dedicated or Private deployment commitment due to operational constraints.
- Customer-Managed Keys (CMK): available for encryption-at-rest for hosted enterprise customers; with CMK we will encrypt payloads with a customer key — deletion requires cryptographic key destruction per agreed process.

Caveats / legal holds:
- We cannot delete data subject to a lawful hold imposed by a court or governmental order. If a hold is presented, deletion will be paused and we will notify the customer with the hold reference.

Attachments I suggest we include in the customer reply: a short one-page deletion runbook (hosted-erasure-playbook.pdf) and a template Data Deletion Certificate. I uploaded both to the drive link in the metadata.

Ben
Ben Carter
Principal Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment stubs: hosted-erasure-playbook.pdf (PDF, 1.1 MB), data-deletion-certificate-template.docx (DOCX, 35 KB)

From: Sofia Ivanova <sofia@redwood.com>

Subject: RE: Customer Security Questionnaire — Data Tenure & Erasure Commitments (Hosted API)

Alex / Dana — thanks for the questionnaire. Below is our consolidated response you can include in your RFP. I've attached a short runbook and a sample signed deletion certificate for review.

Consolidated answers (hosted API):
1) Default retention windows: request/response payloads are retained for 30 calendar days by default. Observability traces and aggregated metrics are retained for 90 days. KV/prefix caches used for inference are ephemeral and follow configured TTLs (seconds–minutes).

2) Deletion process & timeline:
- Initiation: Customer submits deletion request via Console or support ticket indicating tenant id and scope.
- Verification: Redwood verifies requestor identity within 24 business hours.
- Active deletion: Data in active stores is deleted within 5 business days post-verification.
- Backups: Standard hosted backups have a 7-day rolling retention. Deleted data will no longer be present in new backups after active deletion; historical backups are aged/overwritten on the regular 7-day cadence.
- Evidence: A signed Data Deletion Certificate (PDF) is provided within 7 business days after deletion is completed.

Enterprise options (summarized):
- Zero-retention addendum (available on Dedicated/Private or enterprise-hosted addendum): reduces payload persistence to 0 days; requires operational acceptance testing and a minimum committed term.
- Customer-Managed Keys (CMK): available for encryption-at-rest; deletion workflows can incorporate key revocation steps where appropriate.

Caveats: legal holds and regulatory orders may supersede deletion timelines. Some minimal telemetry (routing ids, non-payload metrics) may be retained for short periods to support incident response unless explicitly contracted out.

Please see the attached runbook for the step-by-step operator actions and the template deletion certificate. If you want tightened backup retention or CMK right away, let us know and we can route to Commercial for an amendment and to Engineering for feasibility and scheduling.

preview:
From: Hannah Schultz <hannah.schultz@medtechlabs.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-28T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Follow-up from this morning's demo — streaming concerns and next info

Hi Avery,

Thanks again for the demo today — Tom and I appreciated seeing the streaming preview and the low-latency routing examples. A quick recap from our side and a few asks so we can scope an internal POC: 

- Primary goal: real-time transcription/summary assistant embedded in our clinician dashboard (avg prompt length ~80-200 tokens, median 150).
- Observed issues during the demo: partial output coherence when the model emitted early stop tokens (we saw truncated sentences and stray punctuation) and a few moments where the stream lagged by ~800ms under burst loads.
- Security: any PII/PHI handling notes? We'll need KMS/HSM integration and SOC 2 evidence as part of procurement.

Could you share: 
1) A recommended shortlist of models you think fit low-latency streaming for this workload (we care about coherence under incremental decoding).
2) Any benchmark links or a short report showing latency and partial-output quality for those models (especially under variable seq lengths).
3) Example prompt sets and stop-sequence guidance you recommend for clinician-facing output.

Tom and I are aiming to get a short internal note to procurement by next Thursday (Sept 2). If you can send the shortlist + benchmark links and a sample promptset this week, that would be perfect.

Thanks —
Hannah Schultz
Product Manager, Clinical Tools
MedTechLabs

Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
avery.johnson@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0142

From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.ai>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-31T10:30:00-07:00
Subject: Internal: MedTechLabs — shortlist + streaming UX notes (prep for tech sync)

Team — internal notes ahead of the technical sync with MedTechLabs (tentatively next Tue). High-level findings from the demo and our recommended actions: 

Context: clinician dashboard use-case, median prompt ~150 tokens, tolerance for tail latency <= 200ms. Prospect flagged partial sentence truncation when stop-sequences triggered during streaming; also observed ~700-900ms lag during bursts on the demo rig.

Recommended model shortlist (prioritized):
- redwood-lite-100M-stream (low-latency, optimized kernels) — best for 1st-pass streaming, strong prefix coherence when configured with token-level speculative decoding.
- redwood-2-7b-q4f16 (balanced) — better long-form coherence but ~1.5x token cost; good as a canary fallback when lite model fails quality checks.
- redwood-4-14b (fallback) — highest quality, higher latency/cost; use for post-session summary generation (non-streamed).

Key UX/regression observations and mitigations:
- Partial output coherence: often appears when decoding stops prematurely due to aggressive stop tokens or when quantized kernels mis-handle soft-stop heuristics. Mitigation: tune stop-sequence list to prefer sentence-final punctuation and add a 50-token soft buffer before enforcing hard stops for streaming.
- Speculative decoding: enable 1-step speculative decoding to fill in token gaps for prefix outputs — improves perceived coherence with <10% latency overhead.
- KV cache warmup & prefix caching: warm the KV cache with the clinician prompt template; this reduces initial-token jitter.
- Batching policy: use continuous small-batching (max batch size 4, time window 20ms) to balance latency and throughput for their expected traffic profile.

Subject: Internal: MedTechLabs — shortlist + streaming UX notes (prep for tech sync)

Team — internal notes ahead of the technical sync with MedTechLabs (tentatively next Tue). High-level findings from the demo and our recommended actions: 

Context: clinician dashboard use-case, median prompt ~150 tokens, tolerance for tail latency <= 200ms. Prospect flagged partial sentence truncation when stop-sequences triggered during streaming; also observed ~700-900ms lag during bursts on the demo rig.

Recommended model shortlist (prioritized):
- redwood-lite-100M-stream (low-latency, optimized kernels) — best for 1st-pass streaming, strong prefix coherence when configured with token-level speculative decoding.
- redwood-2-7b-q4f16 (balanced) — better long-form coherence but ~1.5x token cost; good as a canary fallback when lite model fails quality checks.
- redwood-4-14b (fallback) — highest quality, higher latency/cost; use for post-session summary generation (non-streamed).

Key UX/regression observations and mitigations:
- Partial output coherence: often appears when decoding stops prematurely due to aggressive stop tokens or when quantized kernels mis-handle soft-stop heuristics. Mitigation: tune stop-sequence list to prefer sentence-final punctuation and add a 50-token soft buffer before enforcing hard stops for streaming.
- Speculative decoding: enable 1-step speculative decoding to fill in token gaps for prefix outputs — improves perceived coherence with <10% latency overhead.
- KV cache warmup & prefix caching: warm the KV cache with the clinician prompt template; this reduces initial-token jitter.
- Batching policy: use continuous small-batching (max batch size 4, time window 20ms) to balance latency and throughput for their expected traffic profile.

Benchmarks attached: redwood_streaming_benchmarks_aug26.csv — contains latency p50/p95, partial-output coherence scores (custom metric), and token-cost estimates across seq lengths 64/128/256. Prompt sets: prompt_sets_medtech_v1.json (internal) — includes clinician phrasing, stop sequence candidates, and evaluation prompts.

Ask: Lauren/Marcus please review the prompt set and the stop-sequence heuristics; Marcus, can you prepare a 20-min runbook we can walk MedTech through showing how to toggle speculative decoding and prefix caching in Redwood Console?

Thanks —
Avery

Hi Samantha —

Great to e-meet you. Per our procurement intake call, I wanted to kick off formal collection of the items our finance and legal teams need to get BrightLeaf set up as an approved supplier and to enable POs and electronic payments. To keep this tidy, below is a checklist of what we typically need to complete vendor activation and PO routing: 

- Legal entity full name and DBA (if applicable)
- W-9 (or applicable tax form for non-US entities) with EIN/Tax ID clearly visible
- Remit-to address (street, city, state, zip)
- Bank details for ACH and wire (bank name, account number, routing / IBAN, SWIFT) and confirmation if ACH is allowed for initial payments
- Copy of bank letter or bank-originated voided check for account verification (preferred for wires/ACH)
- Preferred invoice receipt email and invoice formatting requirements (PO number field)
- Any payment terms requested (we typically operate net-30; note early-pay discount requests up front)
- Certificate of insurance (COI) if available — our legal team will advise if required for the engagement

If you can share the W-9 and bank verification PDF, we can start the vendor record in our ERP and issue an initial PO template for signature. Attaching the PO template we’ll use for reference.

title: FinSecure onboarding: SSO/SCIM, ephemeral service accounts & IP allowlist sequencing
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Asha Menon <asha.menon@finsecure.com>
Cc: Rajiv Singh <rajiv.singh@finsecure.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: FinSecure onboarding: SSO/SCIM, ephemeral service accounts & IP allowlist sequencing

Asha — great to meet you on Friday. Per our kickoff, I put together a suggested sequencing for access controls during onboarding so we don’t block the first-week integration work. High-level plan attached (redwood_onboarding_sequence_v1.pdf).

Quick summary:
- Week 1 (P0): SSO via SAML, basic role mappings, test users in staging.
- Week 2 (P1): SCIM provisioning for user sync + group mappings.
- Week 3 (P1): Service account pattern — ephemeral SA for CI/CD and a small set of long-lived read-only SA for monitoring.
- Week 4 (P2): IP allowlist for production endpoints, ops runbook and rollback.

Ask: can your security team confirm the IP range(s) we should request and whether SCIM will be enabled for all org units? If you prefer, I can loop in Monica and Jordan to draft the concrete actions and evidence pack for your SOC review.

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: redwood_onboarding_sequence_v1.pdf (PDF)]

From: Asha Menon <asha.menon@finsecure.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Rajiv Singh <rajiv.singh@finsecure.com>, FinSecure Security <security@finsecure.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FinSecure onboarding: SSO/SCIM, ephemeral service accounts & IP allowlist sequencing

Ben — thanks for the clear plan and the attachment. Rajiv and I reviewed briefly; security wants to validate the service account model and see a mapping of which tokens will be long-lived vs ephemeral. Also, SOC asked for an evidence spreadsheet for: SAML metadata, SCIM endpoint ACLs, IP CIDR list, and service account key rotation policy.

Can you provide the evidence template? Also note: FinSecure requires IP ranges be narrowed to two CIDR blocks per vendor (we can expand later). Our SCIM rollout will start with HR and Engineering OUs only.

Cc'ing our security alias so they can add any must-have controls.

—Asha
Asha Menon
Head of Partnerships | FinSecure
[Inline: Rajiv suggests an architecture call Wed AM]


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2026 14:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: FinSecure onboarding: SSO/SCIM, ephemeral service accounts & IP allowlist sequencing

Forwarding FinSecure notes — they want an evidence spreadsheet and confirmation on SA rotation. Please prepare:
1) A one-page pattern doc that explains our ephemeral SA model (how token issuance/rotation works for ephemeral vs long-lived).
2) A prefilled evidence workbook matching their ask (SAML metadata, SCIM ACLs, IP CIDR, rotation logs). Use finsecure-evidence-request.xlsx as a template.
3) A concise checklist of actions we need from FinSecure (SAML metadata, two CIDR blocks, list of HR/Eng OU IDs).

Jordan — can you be on the Wed AM architecture call? Monica — I'll own the SOC evidence pack and coordinating responses. Neha — please confirm whether we can provide a 30-day log export for rotation events from our staging environment.

Thanks — I’m proposing Wed 09:30 PT for the call; Asha/Rajiv indicated availability. If that works I’ll send a calendar invite and include the preliminary evidence workbook.

Best,
Ben
[Attachment: finsecure-evidence-request.xlsx (Excel)]

From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>

- Logs: Neha confirms we can export rotation events from staging for the prior 30 days and will export as CSV by Thu EOD.
- IP ranges: Asha/Rajiv — once you confirm the two CIDR blocks we’ll whitelist them in staging and cut a PR to production allowlist during the Week 4 window.

title: Quick demo & intake questions — Marketly signup
preview:
From: Timothy Park <tim@marketly.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-08-29T15:22:00Z
Subject: Interested in Redwood — quick demo?

Hi Ava,

Thanks — we just signed up for a trial via the self-serve flow (Marketly account: ops@marketly.ai). I'd love to get a short walkthrough of the product and see whether Redwood is a fit for our embedding + assistant use case.

Super-brief background:
- Use case: customer support knowledge base search + assistant (embeddings + reranking).
- Team: AI platform + product (2 engineers, 1 ML infra).
- Timeline: hoping to kick a pilot in early September and evaluate for a Q4 production push.

Can someone on your team send available times next week? Also curious whether you provide a sandbox with a small sample dataset so our engineers can validate latency and output quality.

Thanks — Tim

--
Timothy Park
Head of AI, Marketly
ops@marketly.ai
https://marketly.ai


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Timothy Park <tim@marketly.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-08-29T17:05:00Z
Subject: Re: Interested in Redwood — quick demo?

Hi Tim — great to meet you and glad you signed up. I'm Ava on the solutions team; I'll run point for the demo and intake. A couple quick questions so we tailor the session and make the time valuable:

1) Primary objective: is the priority retrieval relevance (search) or multi-turn assistant behavior? If both, which is higher priority?
2) Estimated monthly volume: approximate API requests or token counts you expect during a pilot and at production scale?
3) Peak concurrency: any SLA/latency targets we should know about (e.g., sub-100ms p50 for short queries)?
4) Timeline: you mentioned early September pilot — is that a two-week pilot or longer evaluation window?
5) Security/compliance: do you need SSO (Okta/SAML), specific data residency, or KMS/HSM integrations for keys?
6) Sandbox: what data sample would be most useful (KB dump, CSV of tickets, vector index snapshot)?

Proposed slots for a 30-minute demo + intake call (PT):
- Thu Sep 2 — 11:00 AM PT
- Thu Sep 2 — 2:00 PM PT
- Fri Sep 3 — 10:00 AM PT

You can also pick a time directly from my calendar: https://cal.redwoodinference.com/ava/book

I've attached a short one-pager (redwood-one-pager.pdf) and a tiny intake form link (demo-intake-form-link.txt) to accelerate the technical prep — if you can fill the form ahead of the call we'll be able to surface a tailored demo.

If you prefer, I can loop in Ben (cc'd) for any deeper infra / throughput questions.

Best,
Ava
SDR, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwoodinference.com
--

On Sun, Aug 29, 2026 at 17:05, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> Hi Tim — great to meet you and glad you signed up. I'm Ava on the solutions team; I'll run point for the demo and intake. A couple quick questions so we tailor the session and make the time valuable:
> 
> 1) Primary objective: is the priority retrieval relevance (search) or multi-turn assistant behavior? If both, which is higher priority?
> 2) Estimated monthly volume: approximate API requests or token counts you expect during a pilot and at production scale?
> 3) Peak concurrency: any SLA/latency targets we should know about (e.g., sub-100ms p50 for short queries)?
> 4) Timeline: you mentioned early September pilot — is that a two-week pilot or longer evaluation window?
> 5) Security/compliance: do you need SSO (Okta/SAML), specific data residency, or KMS/HSM integrations for keys?
> 6) Sandbox: what data sample would be most useful (KB dump, CSV of tickets, vector index snapshot)?
> 
> Proposed slots for a 30-minute demo + intake call (PT):
> - Thu Sep 2 — 11:00 AM PT
> - Thu Sep 2 — 2:00 PM PT
> - Fri Sep 3 — 10:00 AM PT

Thanks, Ava — answers below and happy to take the 11am PT slot on Sep 2.

- Primary objective: search relevance first (we want help surfacing exact KB sections), secondarily a short assistant for triage.
- Pilot volume: roughly 40k requests / month in pilot, ~1.2M requests / month at scale (ballpark).
- Tokens: pilot ~120k tokens/month, production ~3.5M tokens/month.
- Peak concurrency: expect short bursts up to ~250 concurrent requests during business hours.
- Timeline: target pilot kickoff the week of Sep 6 (2–4 week pilot), production evaluation through Q4.
- Security: Okta SSO required for console access; would like KMS support for customer-managed keys long term.
- Sandbox: a 10–15k ticket KB dump (CSV) would be ideal so our engineers can run latency checks; sample vector index also helpful.

Please go ahead and book Sep 2 at 11:00 AM PT. Also, Ben — can you confirm if a small sandbox index is something we can provision for Marketly ahead of the meeting?

—Tim
Timothy Park
Head of AI, Marketly

Subject: Meadow MSA redline — indemnity and defense concerns

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Elena Morales <elena@meadow.com>, Tom Briggs <tom.briggs@meadow.com>
Date: Mon, 31 Aug 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Meadow MSA redline — indemnity and defense concerns

Ben / Jordan — I reviewed Meadow's redline and drafted two discrete proposal texts we can table with procurement. I've pasted them below so procurement can see precise language; if they prefer tracked changes, we can circulate a single redline doc.

Option A — Hosted-defense, insurance-first cap (preferred compromise):
\"Redwood shall defend Customer against any third-party claim alleging that Redwood's hosted service (as provided by Redwood under this Agreement) directly infringes a third party's intellectual property rights, provided that (i) Customer notifies Redwood in writing promptly upon becoming aware of such claim, (ii) Customer grants Redwood sole control of the defense and settlement of such claim, and (iii) Redwood is provided reasonable cooperation and all relevant technical artifacts. Redwood's aggregate liability for indemnification under this Section shall be capped at $5,000,000. The Parties agree that any recovery under Customer's applicable insurance policies shall be applied before Redwood's indemnity cap is triggered (insurance-first waterfall).\"

Option B — Reimburse for customer-controlled environments (BYO/On-Prem):
\"To the extent any claim arises from Customer-provided models, customer datasets, or Customer-hosted deployments (including on-premises or VPC-managed deployments), Redwood's obligation shall be limited to reimbursement of documented defense costs and settlements directly attributable to Redwood's breach, subject to a cap of $1,000,000. Customer shall have the option to control the defense for Customer-controlled environments, provided Redwood may participate and seek reimbursement as set forth above. For these scenarios, the Parties will memorialize technical evidence collection timelines and chain-of-custody procedures in an attached Order Form SOW (Evidence SOW).\"

Key operational asks to include in the MSA/Order Form:
- Notice window: Customer must give Redwood written notice within 30 days of becoming aware of a third-party claim; Redwood will have 10 business days to request technical artifacts necessary to evaluate the claim.
- Evidence SOW: Standard artifact list (conversation logs, prompt + prompt metadata, model version, inference logs, KV cache snapshot where possible) and custodian contact for production of artifacts within 15 business days.
- Subrogation: Redwood reserves the right to pursue subrogation against third parties responsible for the infringement where appropriate.

I recommend we present Option A for the hosted use-case and Option B for BYO/on-prem. If Meadow pushes for defend on all environments, propose a higher cap ($10M) and a specific carveout that excludes claims arising from Customer-supplied training data or prompts.

If Ben is comfortable, I can prepare a tracked-redline MSA showing Option A/B and a short Order Form SOW stub.

Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel, Contracts & Privacy
Redwood Inference

Attachments: none (draft text inline).


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena@meadow.com>, Tom Briggs <tom.briggs@meadow.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 31 Aug 2026 15:48:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Meadow MSA redline — indemnity and defense concerns

Elena / Tom — appreciate the quick review. Kimberly and I discussed and below is a single package proposal to present to Meadow procurement / counsel tomorrow. We think this addresses Meadow's preservation & control needs while limiting Redwood's open-ended exposure.

Proposal (package):
1) Hosted deployments (Redwood-managed): Redwood will defend as per Option A with a $5M indemnity cap and an insurance-first waterfall. Redwood will have primary control of defense but will accept a process for customer objections to settlement if requested in writing (standard reasonableness test).
2) Customer-hosted / BYO: Reimbursement-only as per Option B with $1M cap and the Evidence SOW attached to the Order Form. Customer may elect to assume defense; Redwood may reimburse documented costs if Customer controls defense.
3) Evidence SOW: 30-day notice for initial claim, Redwood may request technical artifacts within 10 business days, and the customer must produce listed artifacts within 15 business days. Custodian contact and transfer method (secure export link) will be identified in the Order Form.
4) Subrogation and cooperation: Redwood reserves subrogation rights and both Parties will cooperate in any third-party recovery efforts.

If that reads OK to Meadow, we'll circulate a tracked-change MSA and a short SOW. If Meadow wants defend-on-all-environments, we'll need to revisit the cap to $10M and confirm insurance treaty limits.

Elena — can you confirm tomorrow morning whether Meadow would accept this package or wants defend everywhere? If defend-everywhere, we can propose the $10M cap and insurance-first but will need procurement sign-off internally.

Happy to hop on a quick call today at 4pm PT to walk through.

Regards,
Ben Carter
Account Exec — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference

title: Embedding & rerank — index scale, SLO targets, and proposed playbook
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Tom Reynolds <tom@searchlytics.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 30 Aug 2026 15:10:00 -0700
Subject: Embedding & rerank — index scale, SLO targets, and proposed playbook

Hi Tom / Ivy,

Thanks again for the time today — good discussion. This thread captures the quick notes I promised and a pragmatic set of next steps so your engineering and product teams can scope the work. If anything below is off, flag it and I’ll adjust.

Short summary of the problem you described:
- SearchLytics has a primary product search index of ~180M documents and a secondary fresh index of ~7M documents for recency queries.
- Current semantic layer uses a managed vector DB with 1536-d embeddings but most queries need sub-50ms p95 latency for interactive UX.
- Monthly query volume averages ~45M embeddings lookups with strong daily and weekly burst patterns tied to campaigns.
- Cost is a material concern: you target a 3–4x reduction in per-query cost compared to current managed endpoint spend.

Success criteria we discussed:
- p95 end-to-end rerank latency <= 60ms for short queries in us-west region (target 40–50ms ideally)
- 99th percentile tail for heavy bursts not to exceed 200ms with graceful fallbacks
- Unit cost per query reduced by at least 50% in steady-state, with predictable cost under bursts
- Accurate top-5 relevance for rerank (you’ll share a small eval set)

Current stack you shared:
- Primary store: Postgres for metadata + Elasticsearch for inverted retrieval
- Semantic store: Managed vector DB (proprietary) for dense vectors, 1536 dimensions
- Orchestration: Kubernetes + Airflow for batch offline embedding updates

Key technical constraints:
- Index size and update cadence: full re-embed monthly, incremental embedding of hot docs hourly (approx 20k/hr)
- Data residency: all inference traffic must remain in us-west cloud region; audit logging required for queries hitting PII buckets
- Tooling preference: your infra team prefers infrastructure we can operate via IaC and tight observability hooks

Attachments: [attachment: search-query-samples.csv (csv)]

Proposed immediate next steps (owner and timing):
1) Action: Send a 500–1k query eval set + labels (you) — target: Tue 2026-09-02
2) Action: Redwood to run a quick cost/latency projection for two candidate architectures (vector DB + Redwood hosted variant) and share a 1-page playbook — owner: Aditya — ETA: Fri 2026-09-04
3) Action: Technical deep-dive with your infra + data engineering (30–45m) to validate update patterns and auth constraints — owner: Lauren + Marcus — target week of 9/7
4) Action: Draft SLA and fallback policy options for spikes (Aditya) — ETA: 9/11 (attached draft SLA v1)

If that timeline looks good I’ll start the projection and book the deep-dive. Appreciate you walking through the traffic profile — gives us confidence we can hit latency targets with a hybrid strategy (shard-by-tenancy + hot-cache + quantized models).

Best,
Aditya Rao
Enterprise Solutions
Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com



From: Tom Reynolds <tom@searchlytics.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Cc: Ivy Chen <ivy@searchlytics.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 30 Aug 2026 16:00:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Embedding & rerank — index scale, SLO targets, and proposed playbook

Aditya — thanks for capturing this. A few clarifications and confirmations below: 

- Index numbers correct: 180M cold + 7M hot. Hot docs get frequent re-embeds from streaming updates.
- For latency, 60ms p95 is a hard bar for the primary UX; 40–50ms would be excellent but we need validation under real burst traces.
- Cost target: we want to get to ~50% of current vector-hosting spend on identical query-shaped workloads; we’re comfortable with a short POC that shows realistic throughput.
- Auth/audit: queries that touch the PII bucket must include a token that we can map back to an internal user ID; logs must be retained for 90 days.

We’ll upload the labelled eval set and a day-of-week traffic sample to the Drive link you shared by Tue.

One ask: when you do the projection, can you include a fallback latency/cost column for the option where we keep the managed vector DB as a fallback (no data movement for 5% of queries)? That will help Product signoff.

Thanks — Tom
Tom Reynolds
Head of Product
SearchLytics

To: Tom Reynolds <tom@searchlytics.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>

- Please drop the labelled eval set and the Day-of-Week traffic CSV into the Drive folder by Tue 2026-09-02. I’ll confirm receipt and sanity-check volumes.

2) Redwood projections and playbook (deliverable):
- What you’ll get: a 1-page architecture comparison (hybrid sharded vector DB with local hot-cache vs Redwood-hosted runner + shard), a table with expected p50/p95 latency estimates for each option, and a cost projection for steady-state + 95th-percentile burst. I’ll add a fallback-row that keeps the managed DB for 5% of traffic as requested.
- Attachments: SLA-draft-v1.pdf (initial wording on SLOs, logging, and retention) — please review and call out any hard stops.
- ETA: Fri 2026-09-04 EOD.

3) Technical deep-dive scheduling
- Lauren and Marcus will run the 45m session. Proposed windows: 2026-09-08 10:00–11:00 PDT or 2026-09-09 13:00–14:00 PDT. Marcus will reach out with a calendar invite once you confirm preferred slot.

4) POC proposal (optional, next phase)
- A small POC we typically run: ingest a 5–10M shard subset, run live QPS at your burst multiplier (x3) to validate tail behavior, and provide perf counters + cost bake-off. If Product wants this, we can scope in the deep-dive.

Next concrete asks for you to unblock us:

If you approve I’ll send an invite and start the projection work.

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 28 Aug 2026 14:40:00 -0700
Subject: Re: INTERNAL: Request: control-plane trust domain decomposition & evidence

Ben / Tessa — attaching a first draft of the artifacts I mentioned. Notes below and next steps.

Attachments included: control-plane-trust-domains_v1.drawio.pdf, segregation_matrix_v1.csv

Key points for the one-page narrative (summary):
- Control vs data plane: control plane is logically separated from inference data plane. For Dedicated deployments we run a customer-dedicated management plane (containerized control services and control-plane proxies) in a separate VPC and dedicated GPU backbone; Private deployments use a customer-managed VPC with a seeded control-plane sidecar that is only reachable over a customer-configured VPN or private interconnect.
- Network boundaries: management/control-plane endpoints are restricted by AWS Security Groups + route table policies; no tenant workloads have direct route entries to control-plane subnets. Inter-region control-plane communication uses encrypted mTLS with strict cert pinning and restricted peering only to Redwood-operated control endpoints.
- KMS scoping: we use per-customer KMS keys for data-encryption-at-rest in Dedicated; for Private we provide a KMS key-rotation/SCOPED config that ties to the customer's external KMS (when requested). KMS audit trail is captured by CloudAudit and retained according to the contract SLA.
- Telemetry & audit: control-plane API gateway emits structured audit logs (timestamped, caller-id, action) to the central logging cluster; relevant excerpts are in the segregation matrix and the log snippets Tessa will add.
- Emergency isolation: tenant eviction/runbook: we maintain a documented playbook (evict-tenant-runbook.md) that lists the step-by-step network egress block, KMS key-disable, and deployment rollback actions. We'll export a sanitized copy.

Requests / open items:
- Tessa: please add the KMS audit snippet and a redacted control-plane API auth log (curl GET /v1/admin/...) to the CSV.
- Ben: confirm whether Helio wants the diagrams as PDF only or the original drawio for edits.

I'll iterate on the narrative once we have the log snippets.

—Kimberly

[Attachment: control-plane-trust-domains_v1.drawio.pdf (application/pdf)]
[Attachment: segregation_matrix_v1.csv (text/csv)]

What we've attached:
- control_plane_decomposition_summary.pdf — one-page narrative covering control-plane vs data-plane and per-deployment notes (Dedicated vs Private).
- control-plane-trust-domains_v1.drawio.pdf — annotated diagrams (per-region overlays) + a native drawio source on request.

- segregation_matrix_log_snippets.txt — redacted audit snippets (KMS access, control-plane API auth events).

Short summary:
- Dedicated: tenant-dedicated control-plane stacks run in isolated VPCs with dedicated GPU pools; management APIs are reachable only via the management VPC and are protected with mTLS + RBAC. KMS keys are per-tenant (Redwood-managed or customer-provided in Private).
- Private: control-plane proxies live inside the customer VPC; control-channel is established over a customer-managed VPN or private interconnect, and Redwood's management APIs are reachable only from the proxied endpoints.
- Evidence highlights: KMS audit logs (redacted), route table snapshots showing no tenant->control-plane routes, drawio diagrams with arrows showing control-channel enforcement, and the segregation CSV mapping to SIG rows.

- Telemetry & audit: control-plane API gateway emits structured audit logs (timestamped, caller-id, action) to the central logging cluster; relevant excerpts are in the segregation matrix and the log snippets Tessa will add.
- Emergency isolation: tenant eviction/runbook: we maintain a documented playbook (evict-tenant-runbook.md) that lists the step-by-step network egress block, KMS key-disable, and deployment rollback actions. We'll export a sanitized copy.

Requests / open items:
- Tessa: please add the KMS audit snippet and a redacted control-plane API auth log (curl GET /v1/admin/...) to the CSV.
- Ben: confirm whether Helio wants the diagrams as PDF only or the original drawio for edits.

I'll iterate on the narrative once we have the log snippets.

—Kimberly

[Attachment: control-plane-trust-domains_v1.drawio.pdf (application/pdf)]
[Attachment: segregation_matrix_v1.csv (text/csv)]

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Samira Jaffer <samira@helio-sec.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 31 Aug 2026 09:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: control-plane trust domain decomposition & evidence

Samira — attaching our initial evidence packet per your SIG rows. Summary below and next steps.

What we've attached:
- control_plane_decomposition_summary.pdf — one-page narrative covering control-plane vs data-plane and per-deployment notes (Dedicated vs Private).
- control-plane-trust-domains_v1.drawio.pdf — annotated diagrams (per-region overlays) + a native drawio source on request.
- segregation_matrix_v1.csv — row-level mapping from SIG/CAIQ to artifacts and log excerpts.
- segregation_matrix_log_snippets.txt — redacted audit snippets (KMS access, control-plane API auth events).

Short summary:
- Dedicated: tenant-dedicated control-plane stacks run in isolated VPCs with dedicated GPU pools; management APIs are reachable only via the management VPC and are protected with mTLS + RBAC. KMS keys are per-tenant (Redwood-managed or customer-provided in Private).
- Private: control-plane proxies live inside the customer VPC; control-channel is established over a customer-managed VPN or private interconnect, and Redwood's management APIs are reachable only from the proxied endpoints.
- Evidence highlights: KMS audit logs (redacted), route table snapshots showing no tenant->control-plane routes, drawio diagrams with arrows showing control-channel enforcement, and the segregation CSV mapping to SIG rows.

Next steps: if Helio's reviewer wants a short walkthrough, Kimberly and I can join a 30-minute call to walk the packet and answer follow-ups. Please propose a few times.

Ben Carter
Account Executive, Security
Redwood Inference

- Completed vulnerability management section (SAST, DAST, third-party dependency scanning cadence)
- Patch SLA commitments for customer-impacting vulnerabilities
- Pen test executive summary and remediation status
- SBOM or bill-of-materials for any runtime components we will run in production

Elena Morales

elena.morales@horizoncap.com

- We will provide SAST/DAST cadence details and recent pen test exec summary.
- SBOM: we can provide a component-level CSV for our hosted runtime (redwood_sbom_v1.csv).

Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com

To: Elena Morales <elena.morales@horizoncap.com>

Date: 2026-08-26T14:20:00-07:00
Subject: Redwood VM responses & evidence (SAST/DAST, patch SLA, SBOM, pentest)

Elena — attaching the artifacts we discussed. See short summary below and attached files: redwood_sbom_v1.csv, pen_test_summary_2026.pdf, patch_sla_table.csv, sast_dast_process.md.

Summary (high level):
- SAST/DAST: We run GitHub-integrated SAST (static analysis) on all public and private repos on every pull request with a daily scheduled full-scan. DAST is run against staging and pre-prod with weekly authenticated scans for critical services. More detail in sast_dast_process.md.
- Dependency scanning & SBOM: We generate an SBOM per release; the attached CSV corresponds to the current production runtime. SBOM contains direct third-party libs and license info.
- Pen tests: Last third-party pen test was May 2026 by SecureAssess; pen_test_summary_2026.pdf includes findings classified and remediation status (all critical findings remediated). The full vendor report can be shared under an NDA — see pentest_fullreport_202605.pdf (requested).
- Patch SLAs: patch_sla_table.csv outlines timelines: Critical (0-72h), High (3-7 days), Medium (30 days), Low (next maintenance window). We also publish CVE disclosures and customer impact notices on our status page.

If you have specific questionnaire rows you'd like us to map to these artifacts, send the row IDs and we'll return a filled workbook.

Thanks,
Grace O'Connor
Director, Product Security | Redwood Inference
grace@redwood.com

To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.com>

1) Procurement will need the redacted full pen test (vendor report) or a signed NDA template we can execute.

If you can send the NDA and a one-paragraph commitment for the contract team, procurement can move this forward.

Best,
Elena

title: Eos Labs: MAP proforma + acceptance gates (GDoc attached)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alice Park <alice.park@eoslabs.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 28 Aug 2026 15:03:00 -0700
Subject: Eos Labs: MAP proforma + acceptance gates (GDoc attached)

Alice / team —

Thanks again for the call this morning. Per our conversation I’m attaching a short MAP proforma (doc) and a lightweight slides skeleton you can use with your stakeholders. I wanted to suggest a tight 30-day acceptance window with three discrete gates so we can keep the pilot focused:

- Week 0: Tech spike & integration check (API auth, VPC connectivity)
- Week 1–2: Core KPI validation (latency, inference cost per 1k tokens, output stability)
- Week 3–4: Acceptance / go/no-go (security review, docs, SLA sign-off)

Two asks from our side: can you (1) drop in any internal dates or blackout windows in the GDoc I linked, and (2) point to who will own procurement / contract execution on your side so we can align timing?

I’ve left placeholder ownership rows in the proforma. If it helps I can draft a short slide (1–2) that explains acceptance criteria for your CTO and procurement.

Attachments: Eos_MAP_Proforma_v0.3.docx (template), gslides-map-skeleton.pptx
GDoc (editable): https://docs.google.com/document/d/1aBcDeFGhIJk-map-proforma
HubSpot: 004129-EOSEVAL

Next steps I propose: you add comments in the GDoc by Monday, we sync Wed to finalize timeline, and we run the tech spike the following week if CI/CD access is ready.

Thanks — looking forward to co-authoring.

—
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Manager, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0123

title: Initial: GLBA addendum, subprocessors & data residency — EverTrust Bank
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Ken Yamada <ken.yamada@evertrustbank.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-08-30T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Initial: GLBA addendum, subprocessors & data residency — EverTrust Bank

Hi Ken —

Thanks for taking the call yesterday. I wanted to follow up with a concise list of items we typically need to evaluate feasibility for regulated financial customers so procurement/legal can scope an addendum and technical options. I attached a short checklist for your team (redwood-addenda-info-checklist.xlsx).

Key questions for EverTrust:
- Confirm that GLBA addendum is the primary vertical addendum required (any overlap with state-level banking regs to surface?).
- Residency: do you require strict US-only residency (single region), or is US-based cloud with multi-region failover acceptable?
- Deployment model preference: dedicated Redwood Dedicated (reserved GPU pool) vs Redwood Private (VPC deployment / customer-managed infra). Any blockers for a Private VPC pattern?
- Subprocessors: do you need an initial list up front, or is an on-demand roster and 30-day notice acceptable? Any prohibited subprocessors (e.g., offshore support vendors)?
- Controls & evidence: SOC 2 Type II report, pen test summary, encryption at rest/in transit, customer-managed keys (KMS/HSM) — which are mandatory vs preferred?

title: Regional deployment exceptions & data-residency playbook
preview:
From: Olivia Carter <olivia.carter@globebank.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Rajesh Kumar <r.kumar@finsecure.co.uk>
Date: 2026-08-30T09:12:00Z
Subject: Initial: Data residency constraint for EU subsidiaries

Hi Ben —

Thanks for the call earlier. Quick follow-up: GlobeBank legal requires that all transaction-level logs and any PII-derived artifacts for EU customers remain within EU-controlled physical infrastructure. They also asked for a short playbook outlining acceptable exceptions (e.g., cross-region routing in DR, on-prem gateway options, and how audit logs are retained). Can you share a concise policy and whether Redwood can guarantee routing only to EU zones for a named account?

Best,
Olivia
Olivia Carter
Head of Procurement, GlobeBank




From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Olivia Carter <olivia.carter@globebank.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-30T10:22:00Z
Subject: Re: Initial: Data residency constraint for EU subsidiaries

Olivia —

Thanks — attaching our recommended playbook and a short exceptions table. TL;DR: yes, we can pin requests for that account to EU-only execution (hosted or dedicated) and ensure that model binaries and ephemeral runtimes are in the EU. Important caveats included in the attachment:

- Control plane metadata (account config, billing records) is stored in our control plane; for Private deployments we provide a customer-managed control plane option.
- Cross-region failover: we support automatic failover to a secondary EU zone. We do not recommend failing over to non-EU regions unless a documented emergency exception is approved.
- Backups & logs: Nightly backups for transaction logs can be configured to remain in EU storage (SSE + KMS with EU key). Audit logs for infra events can be exported to the customer's EU-only S3 bucket.

I called out three exception patterns in the playbook: 1) DR to non-EU when EU capacity exhausted (requires signed Exception Form), 2) short-lived developer debugging sessions that may route out-of-region (restricted by role and logged), 3) telemetry/eval exports to central analytics (we can provide pseudonymized exports or EU-only pipelines).

Attaching: redwood_residency_playbook_v3.pdf and the exceptions spreadsheet. We can walk the legal team through each line.

Next step: propose a 30-min walkthrough this afternoon if that works for you.

— Ben
Ben Carter
Principal Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Rajesh Kumar <r.kumar@finsecure.co.uk>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Olivia Carter <olivia.carter@globebank.com>
Date: 2026-08-30T12:05:00Z
Subject: Re: Initial: Data residency constraint for EU subsidiaries

On Mon, Aug 30, 2026 at 10:22 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Olivia —
>
> Thanks — attaching our recommended playbook and a short exceptions table. TL;DR: yes, ...

Couple of technical clarifications from our side (Compliance / SecOps):

1) KMS: we need confirmation that customer-managed keys (CMKs) can be hosted in the customer's EU KMS (Azure Key Vault / AWS KMS multi-account) and that Redwood will not have admin access to those CMKs.
2) Model caching: how long does the KV cache persist on the serving nodes? We need an upper bound and an ability to evict per-account caches on-demand.
3) Network egress metadata: can you provide a list of IP ranges and points-of-presence (PoPs) for EU regions so our firewall rules can be scoped?

If Redwood can satisfy these three items in the playbook, we can move to an approval in principle.

— Rajesh
Rajesh Kumar
Lead, Cloud Compliance — FinSecure (consulting, supporting GlobeBank)


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
To: Rajesh Kumar <r.kumar@finsecure.co.uk>, Olivia Carter <olivia.carter@globebank.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-30T13:48:00Z
Subject: Re: Initial: Data residency constraint for EU subsidiaries

Hi Rajesh / Olivia —

Thanks — responding inline to your three asks and copying the playbook sections that apply.

1) KMS: Yes — we support customer-managed keys. For hosted accounts we provide an integration where the customer grants a dedicated RW key permission scoped to encryption/decryption for the account's storage buckets; Redwood never stores plaintext keys and cannot export CMKs. For Private (VPC/on-prem) customers, you retain full key control (no Redwood access). See playbook section 2.1 (KMS integration).

2) KV cache: Our serving nodes keep an account-scoped KV cache to accelerate decoder performance. Default TTL is 24 hours, configurable down to 0 (eviction on every request). We also support on-demand invalidation via API — we can trigger eviction for a single account or all accounts on a region when requested. Note: flushing frequently increases per-request latency and cost.

3) PoPs / IP ranges: Attached is a static list of current EU PoPs and IP ranges used for API egress. We provide a CIDR file that you can ingest; we also offer an allowlist VPN tunneling option for Dedicated and Private customers.

Operationally, the playbook recommends a two-step acceptance for exceptions: (A) emergency rapid-approval (security officer signoff + time-limited exception), (B) post-incident remediation that returns data to EU-only residency. We can include template Exception Forms for your legal team.

Propose 3pm UTC for a 30-min walkthrough — is that viable?

Cheers,
Lauren
Director, Product Compliance (Redwood Inference)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Olivia Carter <olivia.carter@globebank.com>, Rajesh Kumar <r.kumar@finsecure.co.uk>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-30T15:44:00Z
Subject: Re: Initial: Data residency constraint for EU subsidiaries

Olivia / Rajesh —

Thanks for the quick review. Confirming: 15:00 UTC walkthrough accepted and I will share a calendar invite with the playbook and exception form pre-attached. Action items we will cover in the call:

- Walk through KMS flows and confirm CMK integration steps (owner: Lauren)
- Show the eviction API and demonstrate per-account cache purge (owner: Rishi)
- Review the exceptions spreadsheet and signoff workflow (owner: Ben)
- Provide final CIDR / PoP list and a draft VPN allowlist config (owner: Ops)

We will also provide a short SLA note clarifying that routing constraints are enforced at the account routing layer and that non-EU routing requires an explicit exception recorded in HubSpot/DocuSign.

See you at 15:00 UTC.

— Ben
Principal Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference

They also asked for a short playbook outlining acceptable exceptions (e.g., cross-region routing in DR, on-prem gateway options, and how audit logs are retained). Can you share a concise policy and whether Redwood can guarantee routing only to EU zones for a named account?

Best,
Olivia
Olivia Carter
Head of Procurement, GlobeBank




From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Olivia Carter <olivia.carter@globebank.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-30T10:22:00Z
Subject: Re: Initial: Data residency constraint for EU subsidiaries

Olivia —

Thanks — attaching our recommended playbook and a short exceptions table. TL;DR: yes, we can pin requests for that account to EU-only execution (hosted or dedicated) and ensure that model binaries and ephemeral runtimes are in the EU. Important caveats included in the attachment:

- Control plane metadata (account config, billing records) is stored in our control plane; for Private deployments we provide a customer-managed control plane option.
- Cross-region failover: we support automatic failover to a secondary EU zone. We do not recommend failing over to non-EU regions unless a documented emergency exception is approved.
- Backups & logs: Nightly backups for transaction logs can be configured to remain in EU storage (SSE + KMS with EU key). Audit logs for infra events can be exported to the customer's EU-only S3 bucket.

I called out three exception patterns in the playbook: 1) DR to non-EU when EU capacity exhausted (requires signed Exception Form), 2) short-lived developer debugging sessions that may route out-of-region (restricted by role and logged), 3) telemetry/eval exports to central analytics (we can provide pseudonymized exports or EU-only pipelines).

Attaching: redwood_residency_playbook_v3.pdf and the exceptions spreadsheet. We can walk the legal team through each line.

Next step: propose a 30-min walkthrough this afternoon if that works for you.

— Ben
Ben Carter
Principal Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Rajesh Kumar <r.kumar@finsecure.co.uk>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Olivia Carter <olivia.carter@globebank.com>
Date: 2026-08-30T12:05:00Z
Subject: Re: Initial: Data residency constraint for EU subsidiaries

On Mon, Aug 30, 2026 at 10:22 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Olivia —
>
> Thanks — attaching our recommended playbook and a short exceptions table. TL;DR: yes, ...

Couple of technical clarifications from our side (Compliance / SecOps):

1) KMS: we need confirmation that customer-managed keys (CMKs) can be hosted in the customer's EU KMS (Azure Key Vault / AWS KMS multi-account) and that Redwood will not have admin access to those CMKs.
2) Model caching: how long does the KV cache persist on the serving nodes? We need an upper bound and an ability to evict per-account caches on-demand.
3) Network egress metadata: can you provide a list of IP ranges and points-of-presence (PoPs) for EU regions so our firewall rules can be scoped?

If Redwood can satisfy these three items in the playbook, we can move to an approval in principle.

— Rajesh
Rajesh Kumar
Lead, Cloud Compliance — FinSecure (consulting, supporting GlobeBank)


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
To: Rajesh Kumar <r.kumar@finsecure.co.uk>, Olivia Carter <olivia.carter@globebank.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-30T13:48:00Z
Subject: Re: Initial: Data residency constraint for EU subsidiaries

Hi Rajesh / Olivia —

Thanks — responding inline to your three asks and copying the playbook sections that apply.

1) KMS: Yes — we support customer-managed keys. For hosted accounts we provide an integration where the customer grants a dedicated RW key permission scoped to encryption/decryption for the account's storage buckets; Redwood never stores plaintext keys and cannot export CMKs. For Private (VPC/on-prem) customers, you retain full key control (no Redwood access). See playbook section 2.1 (KMS integration).

2) KV cache: Our serving nodes keep an account-scoped KV cache to accelerate decoder performance. Default TTL is 24 hours, configurable down to 0 (eviction on every request). We also support on-demand invalidation via API — we can trigger eviction for a single account or all accounts on a region when requested. Note: flushing frequently increases per-request latency and cost.

3) PoPs / IP ranges: Attached is a static list of current EU PoPs and IP ranges used for API egress. We provide a CIDR file that you can ingest; we also offer an allowlist VPN tunneling option for Dedicated and Private customers.

Operationally, the playbook recommends a two-step acceptance for exceptions: (A) emergency rapid-approval (security officer signoff + time-limited exception), (B) post-incident remediation that returns data to EU-only residency. We can include template Exception Forms for your legal team.

Propose 3pm UTC for a 30-min walkthrough — is that viable?

Cheers,
Lauren
Director, Product Compliance (Redwood Inference)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Olivia Carter <olivia.carter@globebank.com>, Rajesh Kumar <r.kumar@finsecure.co.uk>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-30T15:44:00Z
Subject: Re: Initial: Data residency constraint for EU subsidiaries

Olivia / Rajesh —

Thanks for the quick review. Confirming: 15:00 UTC walkthrough accepted and I will share a calendar invite with the playbook and exception form pre-attached. Action items we will cover in the call:

- Walk through KMS flows and confirm CMK integration steps (owner: Lauren)
- Show the eviction API and demonstrate per-account cache purge (owner: Rishi)
- Review the exceptions spreadsheet and signoff workflow (owner: Ben)
- Provide final CIDR / PoP list and a draft VPN allowlist config (owner: Ops)

We will also provide a short SLA note clarifying that routing constraints are enforced at the account routing layer and that non-EU routing requires an explicit exception recorded in HubSpot/DocuSign.

See you at 15:00 UTC.

— Ben
Principal Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference

[Attachment stubs: redwood_residency_playbook_v3.pdf (15 pages), eu-routing-exceptions-table.xlsx (1 sheet), apac-capacity-notes.txt]

title: Re: Credit corridor and EU withholding clarity
preview:
From: Trevor Hayes <trevor@mediform.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.ai>
Cc: payments@mediform.com
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Request to move to Net60 for Q3 invoices + VAT/withholding note

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the helpful demo last week. We wanted to circle back on payment terms for our pilot period. Our CFO is asking if Mediform can move to Net60 for the initial three invoices (Aug–Oct) while we smooth cashflow ahead of our Medicare billing cycle.

A few constraints from our side:
- We need a credit buffer (small reserve) while on extended terms.
- EU VAT handling: our AP team may withhold VAT on cross-border invoices unless we provide explicit VAT registration evidence.
- We can provide an upfront deposit to offset risk if that helps.

I've attached a proposed payment timeline (mediform_proposed_payment_timeline.xlsx). Can Redwood confirm whether Net60 is acceptable, and what documentation you need for VAT / withholding?

Best,
Trevor Hayes
VP Finance, Mediform Health

--- Attachment: mediform_proposed_payment_timeline.xlsx (spreadsheet) ---

From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.ai>
To: Trevor Hayes <trevor@mediform.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 21 Aug 2026 10:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request to move to Net60 for Q3 invoices + VAT/withholding note

Trevor,

Appreciate the clear ask and the timeline. We're open to accommodating a temporary Net60 for the first three invoices with a couple of guardrails so both sides are covered. High level proposal:

- Net45 by default, but Net60 for Aug–Oct as a time-limited concession (three invoices).
- Mediform provides either a one-time deposit equal to 30% of the first invoice or a rolling credit buffer (we can size this after reviewing usage estimates).
- Redwood requests VAT registration evidence or a UK/EU VAT exemption certificate to avoid local withholding; if not available, we may need to gross-up invoice amounts to be VAT-neutral.
- We will place a conditional credit hold trigger at 45 days past due if the rolling buffer drops below threshold; automatic notification to your AP and our finance ops.

Can Marcus / Laura confirm internal approvals for the temporary Net60 + deposit option? Also attaching Redwood's short credit summary for context (redwood_credit_summary.pdf) and a VAT evidence checklist (vat_evidence_request.docx).

If this looks acceptable, we can draft an amendment to the MSA and a short mutual action plan to capture milestones and the deposit flow.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0142

--- Attachments: redwood_credit_summary.pdf, vat_evidence_request.docx ---

From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 24 Aug 2026 08:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request to move to Net60 for Q3 invoices + VAT/withholding note

Ava / Laura,

I'm OK with the temporary Net60 concession under these conditions:
- Deposit equal to 25–30% of month-one invoice (we should ask for 30% given usage variability).
- A schedule for actual usage reconciliation at the end of the three-month window; any over/under will be credited or billed on the next invoice.
- VAT evidence required within 10 business days of signing the amendment. If not provided, we'll default to net amounts that assume potential withholding.
- Finance to enable a one-click payment link on our invoices and set up an early-pay discount (0.5%) if they pay within 10 days to encourage earlier settlement.

I'll green-light legal to prepare a short amendment with those points and add a clause that allows reverting to Net45 after the pilot unless both parties agree to continue Net60. Laura, please check SLA implications for the credit buffer.

Marcus Lin
Head of Revenue Operations | Redwood Inference
marcus@redwood.ai

--- Note: internal approval, no attachments ---

From: Trevor Hayes <trevor@mediform.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.ai>
Cc: payments@mediform.com, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 29 Aug 2026 16:42:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Credit corridor and EU withholding clarity

Ava / Marcus,

Thanks — this is helpful and fair. Mediform agrees to:
- Provide a 30% deposit on the first invoice.
- Submit VAT registration evidence by 2026-09-10 (we'll upload to the Drive link you shared).
- Accept the reconciliation at the end of the three-month window; please include the early-pay link on invoices.

We'll sign the amendment once Laura's team confirms the SLA note in writing. I've attached a signed copy of the proposed timeline for reference.

Trevor Hayes
VP Finance, Mediform Health

--- Attachment: mediform_signed_timeline.pdf (PDF) ---

title: Aurora Systems — throughput snapshot & request to place staging capacity hold (AUR-4723)
preview:
From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-29T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Quick inventory + soft-hold request for Aurora (AUR-4723)

Team —

Aurora (late-stage, deal AUR-4723) surfaced a confirmed POC sizing today that could convert to an MRC. They want us to guarantee a short exclusive staging window while they complete procurement. Key ask below — can you reply with availability and feasibility within 2 hours?

What Aurora is planning (from discovery):
- Model: Llama2-70B (int8 / Q4-style quantized variant)
- Concurrent workers: 35–45 (they expect burst concurrency for evaluation)
- Target throughput: estimated 2.5–3k tokens/sec sustained during POC peak windows
- Retention: 3–6 months initial reserved capacity; possibility to convert to 12+ mos after commit

Request from me:
1) Quick inventory snapshot (available GPU count by type: A100 80GB, A6000, H100 nodes) that can meet ~3k t/s with acceptable latency (p95 under 250ms).
2) Suggest a minimal configuration we could hard-stake as a soft hold for 2 weeks (i.e., hold without invoicing) and any blockers (network, nvlink ratios, node isolation).
3) If feasible, a shortlist of pricing uplift for reserved staging (monthly rate delta vs on-demand).

Attachments: Aurora_capacity_request_v3.xlsx (attached to external thread later).

I know this is tight — tagging Ben and Chloe because of fleet scheduling and Evelyn on billing/contract impact.

—Rafael
Rafael Mendes
Head of GTM, Redwood Inference
rafael.mendes@redwood.com



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: 2026-08-29T10:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Quick inventory + soft-hold request for Aurora (AUR-4723)

Rafael —

Inventory snapshot (current usable capacity as of this morning):
- H100 (1U) pool: 6 nodes immediately free (each node supports ~400–600 t/s depending on quant and batch).
- A100 80GB: 8 GPUs free across 4 nodes (good for model parallel setups, ~250–350 t/s per GPU).
- A6000: 24 GPUs spread across multi-tenant racks (approx 120–180 t/s per GPU for Q8 quantized path).

Rough throughput scenarios (conservative):
- Option A (latency-optimized): 6x H100 nodes -> est 2.4–3k t/s with p95 ~180–260ms.
- Option B (capacity-optimized): 10x A6000 equivalents -> est 2.0–2.8k t/s p95 ~220–300ms.
- Option C (mixed): 4 H100 + 6 A6000 -> best balance for cost & latency (~2.5–3k).

Soft-hold feasibility:
- We can place a soft staging hold (tag resources) for up to 14 days without reallocating billing, provided we can reserve those nodes from the remaining free pool.
- Blocking items: a) if they require dedicated NVLink fabrics (MP interconnect), we'd need A100 nodes with correct cabling — inventory is limited; b) private VPC interconnect setup could add 48–72 hours to readiness.

Pricing delta: approx +18–28% for short-term reserved staging vs spot/on-demand (depends on node mix). If they convert to a 12-month MRC, the delta drops significantly (we can do numbers once Aurora signals).

Actionable: I can place a soft hold on Option C (4 H100 + 6 A6000) for 14 days. Need confirmation from sales that this is the requested staging footprint.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Fleet Capacity Manager, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com



From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir.patel@aurorasystems.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-29T11:20:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Capacity preview for Aurora POC — proposed staging hold (AUR-4723)

Samir —

Thanks for the details on your side. Quick operational update from our end after an internal check: we have a viable configuration to run your POC at the target throughput. Ben recommended a mixed staging footprint that balances latency and cost: 4x H100 nodes + 6x A6000 GPUs (rough throughput ~2.5–3k tokens/sec, p95 latency in the ~200–280ms range).

Proposal (next steps):
- We’ll place a 14-day soft staging hold on the above footprint while you complete procurement paperwork. This hold does not trigger billing; it ensures we do not reassign those nodes to other customers.
- During the hold we’ll run a short readiness checklist: VPC peering (if required), service account provisioning, and a short warm-up run. Expect ~2 business days to be fully provisioned once network details are finalized.
- Pricing: short-term staging uplift ~+22% vs on-demand. If you convert to a 12-month MRC, we can provide a tiered discount and contract credits for early conversion.

Attached: Aurora_capacity_request_v3.xlsx (for your internal reference) and a draft SLA overview (SLA_Draft_Aurora.pdf). I’ve also added a one-page MAP here: https://drive.google.com/open?id=MAP-AUR-4723.

Please confirm if you want us to proceed with the 14-day soft hold and we’ll put it in place this afternoon. If you want a smaller or larger footprint we can adjust before tagging.

Best,
Rafael Mendes
Head of GTM, Redwood Inference
rafael.mendes@redwood.com



From: Samir Patel <samir.patel@aurorasystems.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@aurorasystems.com
Date: 2026-08-29T12:15:00-04:00
Subject: Re: RE: Capacity preview for Aurora POC — proposed staging hold (AUR-4723)

Rafael —

Thanks for the quick turnaround and the detailed breakdown. The footprint you proposed (4 H100 + 6 A6000) looks right for our initial evaluation. We’d like to request a 14-day exclusivity hold while we finish procurement approvals — during that time we’ll:
- Finalize legal review of the SLA
- Provide network/contact details for provisioning
- Confirm expected start date for the warm-up run (we’re targeting week of Sep 7)

On pricing: please send a baseline MRC offer (12-month) and a short-term staging quote. Procurement is asking specifically for the monthly uplift number and whether there’s any refundable deposit.

We’ll consider moving to an MRC if the POC meets latency and cost objectives.

Regards,
Samir Patel
Dir, AI Infrastructure — Aurora Systems
samir.patel@aurorasystems.com

Date: 2026-08-29T13:00:00-07:00

Ben — I’ve added an operational ticket (SALESAUR-4723) with the provisioning checklist. Can you confirm ETA on infra readiness once Samir sends VPC details (anticipate 48–72 hours)?
